shortDefinition: Honest and direct, even when it’s hard.
longDefinition: Open and truthful in a way that feels sincere rather than blunt or guarded.
example: She was candid about what the project would really cost.
secondExample: I’ll be candid: that plan worries me.
useCase: Use it for honesty that builds trust.
origin: From Latin candidus, “white, sincere,” from candere, “to shine.”

shortDefinition: An easy, trusting connection between people.
longDefinition: A sense of mutual understanding that makes communication feel natural and warm.
example: The new manager built rapport with the team within a week.
secondExample: Good doctors establish rapport before anything else.
useCase: Use it for the chemistry that makes people click.
origin: From French rapporter, “to bring back,” the sense of relating to one another.

shortDefinition: Knowing the kind thing to say, and when.
longDefinition: A sense for handling delicate situations and people without causing offense.
example: She delivered the bad news with real tact.
secondExample: It takes tact to disagree without bruising anyone.
useCase: Use it for graceful handling of touchy moments.
origin: From Latin tactus, “touch,” from tangere, “to touch.”

longDefinition: A composed steadiness, especially under pressure or attention.
example: He answered the tough question with surprising poise.
secondExample: She walked on stage with complete poise.
useCase: Use it for composure that looks effortless.
origin: From Old French pois, “weight,” from Latin pensum — to be balanced.

longDefinition: Naturally outgoing and fond of company, drawing energy from others.
example: Her gregarious nature made the party feel easy.
secondExample: He’s gregarious enough to befriend a whole train carriage.
useCase: Use it for someone who lights up in a crowd.
origin: From Latin gregarius, “of a flock,” from grex, “flock.”

shortDefinition: Easy to talk to and warmly friendly.
longDefinition: Pleasant and approachable, the kind of person conversation flows with.
example: Their affable host put every guest at ease.
secondExample: He’s affable even before his morning coffee.
useCase: Use it for easy, welcoming friendliness.
origin: From Latin affabilis, “easy to speak to,” from affari, “to speak to.”

longDefinition: Showing precise, thorough attention so that nothing is overlooked.
example: Her meticulous notes saved the whole investigation.
secondExample: He’s meticulous about backing up his work.
useCase: Use it for painstaking, detailed care.
origin: From Latin meticulosus, “fearful” — careful to the point of anxiety.

shortDefinition: Sharp at reading people and situations.
longDefinition: Quick to notice what matters and to judge it shrewdly.
example: An astute investor, she sold just before the crash.
secondExample: That was an astute observation about the client.
useCase: Use it for shrewd, perceptive judgment.
origin: From Latin astutus, “crafty,” from astus, “cunning.”

shortDefinition: Hard-working and careful in your effort.
longDefinition: Steady and conscientious about doing a task well over time.
example: Diligent practice turned a shaky start into mastery.
secondExample: She’s diligent about replying within a day.
useCase: Use it for steady, careful effort.
origin: From Latin diligere, “to value, take delight in” — care that comes from caring.

shortDefinition: Guided by a strong sense of duty and care.
longDefinition: Wishing to do what is right and to do every task properly.
example: A conscientious employee, he double-checked every figure.
secondExample: She’s too conscientious to leave a job half-done.
useCase: Use it for principled, careful reliability.
origin: From Latin conscientia, “conscience, knowledge within oneself.”

shortDefinition: Refusing to let go; gritty persistence.
longDefinition: The determination to keep holding on through difficulty.
example: Her tenacity carried the project past every setback.
secondExample: Talent opens the door; tenacity walks through it.
useCase: Use it for determined, stubborn persistence.
origin: From Latin tenax, “holding fast,” from tenere, “to hold.”

shortDefinition: Able to recover quickly from hard things.
longDefinition: Springing back from difficulty, stress, or setback rather than breaking.
example: Kids can be remarkably resilient after a tough year.
secondExample: A resilient team treats failure as information.
useCase: Use it for the strength to bounce back.
origin: From Latin resilire, “to leap back,” from re- + salire, “to jump.”

shortDefinition: Practical, focused on what actually works.
longDefinition: Dealing with things sensibly and realistically rather than ideally.
example: Let’s be pragmatic and ship the simple version first.
secondExample: Her pragmatic approach cut the meeting in half.
useCase: Use it for grounded, results-first thinking.
origin: From Greek pragma, “deed, thing done,” from prassein, “to do.”

longDefinition: Able to adapt or be used for a wide range of tasks or roles.
example: A versatile player, she covers three positions.
secondExample: Olive oil is the most versatile thing in the kitchen.
useCase: Use it for flexible, all-around ability.
origin: From Latin versatilis, “turning, capable of turning,” from vertere, “to turn.”

shortDefinition: Able to express ideas clearly and well.
longDefinition: Speaking or writing in a fluent, coherent, easy-to-follow way.
example: She’s remarkably articulate under pressure.
secondExample: He gave an articulate summary of a messy problem.
useCase: Use it for clear, fluent expression.
origin: From Latin articulare, “to divide into distinct parts,” from articulus, “joint.”

shortDefinition: Saying a lot in few words.
longDefinition: Brief but complete, with nothing wasted.
example: Keep the email concise — three sentences, tops.
secondExample: A concise answer is often the most convincing.
useCase: Use it for brevity that still says everything.
origin: From Latin concidere, “to cut up,” from caedere, “to cut.”

longDefinition: Compressed to the essentials without losing meaning.
example: Her succinct reply settled the debate.
secondExample: Make the title succinct and the point obvious.
useCase: Use it for crisp, compact wording.
origin: From Latin succinctus, “girded up, tucked in” — ready and trim.

shortDefinition: Fluent and moving in speech or writing.
longDefinition: Expressing ideas gracefully and persuasively, with feeling.
example: She gave an eloquent toast that left no dry eyes.
secondExample: His silence was somehow more eloquent than words.
useCase: Use it for graceful, persuasive expression.
origin: From Latin eloqui, “to speak out,” from ex- + loqui, “to speak.”

longDefinition: Light, witty conversation traded for fun rather than to win.
example: Their easy banter made the long drive fly by.
secondExample: There’s a warmth to good banter.
useCase: Use it for friendly, quick-witted teasing.
origin: Of uncertain origin, first recorded in late 1600s London slang.

shortDefinition: A quick, clever, often funny remark.
longDefinition: A witty comment made offhand, usually to amuse.
example: He defused the tension with a well-timed quip.
secondExample: She has a quip ready for everything.
useCase: Use it for a fast, witty one-liner.
origin: Perhaps from Latin quippe, “indeed, of course,” used sarcastically.

longDefinition: The ability to understand and share someone else’s emotions from the inside.
example: Her empathy made hard conversations feel safe.
secondExample: Empathy is listening without rushing to fix.
useCase: Use it for genuinely sharing another’s feeling.
origin: From Greek empatheia, “feeling into,” from en- + pathos, “feeling.”

shortDefinition: A fond, slightly aching longing for the past.
longDefinition: A sentimental yearning for an earlier time or place, sweet and a little sad.
example: The song hit her with a wave of nostalgia.
secondExample: Old photos are pure nostalgia.
useCase: Use it for tender longing for what’s gone.
origin: Coined in 1688 from Greek nostos, “homecoming,” and algos, “pain.”

longDefinition: A pensive low mood that isn’t quite grief — quiet, reflective, almost tender.
example: A soft melancholy settled in as the leaves fell.
secondExample: There’s a beautiful melancholy to autumn evenings.
useCase: Use it for reflective, low-key sadness.
origin: From Greek melas, “black,” and kholē, “bile” — an old theory of the moods.

longDefinition: A soaring, almost overwhelming sense of happiness or elation.
example: Crossing the finish line, she felt pure euphoria.
secondExample: The win sent the whole city into euphoria.
useCase: Use it for joy at its highest pitch.
origin: From Greek euphoria, “power of enduring easily,” from eu-, “well,” + pherein, “to bear.”

longDefinition: Marked by a clear, quiet calm — whether a mood, a face, or a lake.
example: She stayed serene while everyone else panicked.
secondExample: The morning was serene and still.
useCase: Use it for unshakable, quiet calm.
origin: From Latin serenus, “clear, fair, calm,” first of weather.

shortDefinition: Gently longing, with a touch of sadness.
longDefinition: Full of quiet, yearning thoughtfulness about something wished-for or gone.
example: He gave a wistful smile at the old address.
secondExample: She felt wistful watching the last train leave.
useCase: Use it for soft, yearning reflection.
origin: Likely from obsolete wistly, “intently,” reshaped by wishful.

shortDefinition: Uneasy that something might go wrong.
longDefinition: Anxious or fearful about what may happen next.
example: She felt apprehensive before the results came in.
secondExample: He was apprehensive about flying for the first time.
useCase: Use it for nervous worry about the future.
origin: From Latin apprehendere, “to grasp” — to grasp danger in advance.

longDefinition: A person or event that triggers action or speeds things up — in chemistry, without being used up itself.
example: The new hire was a catalyst for the whole team.
secondExample: One bad review became the catalyst for a redesign.
useCase: Use it for the spark that sets things moving.
origin: From Greek katalysis, “dissolution,” from kata-, “down,” + lyein, “to loosen.”

longDefinition: A condition where opposing forces or influences are evenly matched and stable.
example: After the move, life slowly returned to equilibrium.
secondExample: The market found a new equilibrium.
useCase: Use it for a balanced, settled state.
origin: From Latin aequilibrium, from aequus, “equal,” + libra, “balance.”

shortDefinition: A relationship where both sides benefit.
longDefinition: A close, mutually helpful partnership — originally between two living things.
example: The designer and engineer worked in perfect symbiosis.
secondExample: Bees and flowers are a classic symbiosis.
useCase: Use it for a partnership that helps both sides.
origin: From Greek symbiōsis, “living together,” from syn-, “with,” + bios, “life.”

shortDefinition: A remarkable thing that can be observed.
longDefinition: An occurrence or fact, especially one striking enough to study or marvel at.
example: Bioluminescence is one of nature’s strangest phenomena.
secondExample: The band became a global phenomenon overnight.
useCase: Use it for a notable, observable occurrence.
origin: From Greek phainomenon, “thing appearing,” from phainein, “to show.”

shortDefinition: A model or way of seeing things.
longDefinition: A typical pattern or framework that shapes how a field or person thinks.
example: Remote work was a paradigm shift for the company.
secondExample: The discovery overturned the old scientific paradigm.
useCase: Use it for a defining model or mindset.
origin: From Greek paradeigma, “pattern, example,” from paradeiknynai, “to show side by side.”

shortDefinition: Based on observation and evidence, not theory.
longDefinition: Derived from real experiment or experience rather than pure reasoning.
example: There’s no empirical support for that claim yet.
secondExample: She prefers empirical data to gut feeling.
useCase: Use it for conclusions grounded in evidence.
origin: From Greek empeiria, “experience,” from en- + peira, “trial.”

longDefinition: A measure of disorder in a system; informally, the way things tend to fall apart without effort.
example: Left alone, the garage succumbs to entropy.
secondExample: Fighting entropy is half of running a team.
useCase: Use it for the natural slide into disorder.
origin: Coined in 1865 from Greek entropia, “a turning toward,” to mirror “energy.”

shortDefinition: The mood and spirit of a particular time.
longDefinition: The defining ideas, beliefs, and feelings of an era.
example: The film perfectly captured the zeitgeist of the 90s.
secondExample: That slang is pure current zeitgeist.
useCase: Use it for the spirit of an age.
origin: From German Zeit, “time,” + Geist, “spirit.”

shortDefinition: A revival or rebirth, especially of culture.
longDefinition: A renewed flourishing of art, ideas, or activity after a quiet period.
example: The neighborhood is enjoying a real renaissance.
secondExample: His late albums were a creative renaissance.
useCase: Use it for a vivid revival or comeback.
origin: From French renaissance, “rebirth,” from Latin renasci, “to be born again.”

shortDefinition: A subtle difference in meaning or feeling.
longDefinition: A fine distinction that changes how something should be understood.
example: The debate needed more nuance and less certainty.
secondExample: A single word of nuance can shift the whole tone.
useCase: Use it for subtle, telling distinctions.
origin: From French nuance, “shade of color,” from nue, “cloud,” via Latin nubes.

longDefinition: The quality of being sincere and truthful without unnecessary harshness.
example: Her candor made the feedback easy to trust.
secondExample: Teams move faster when candor feels safe.
useCase: Use it for clean, kind honesty.
origin: From Latin candor, “whiteness, sincerity,” from candere, “to shine.”

longDefinition: A source of relief or consolation that softens grief or worry.
example: He found solace in a long walk after the call.
secondExample: The familiar song offered unexpected solace.
useCase: Use it for gentle, steadying comfort.
origin: From Latin solacium, “comfort,” from solari, “to console.”

longDefinition: Steady in intention, especially when it would be easier to give up.
example: She stayed resolute after the first rejection.
secondExample: A resolute tone can calm an uncertain team.
useCase: Use it for determined steadiness.
origin: From Latin resolutus, past participle of resolvere, “to loosen, settle.”

shortDefinition: Clear, bright, and easy to understand.
longDefinition: Expressed with clean clarity, especially when an idea could easily have been muddled.
example: His explanation was so lucid the whole room relaxed.
secondExample: She wanted her writing to feel lucid, not overworked.
useCase: Use it to praise clear thinking or writing.
origin: From Latin lucidus, “bright,” from lucere, “to shine.”

shortDefinition: Too great or subtle to put into words.
longDefinition: So intense, strange, or beautiful that ordinary language cannot quite hold it.
example: There was an ineffable comfort in hearing her old street name.
secondExample: The view had an ineffable stillness.
useCase: Use it for feelings that resist explanation.
origin: From Latin ineffabilis, “unspeakable,” from in-, “not,” + effabilis, “utterable.”

shortDefinition: The realization that every stranger has a life as vivid as your own.
longDefinition: The sudden awareness that each passerby is living a story as complex and meaningful as yours.
example: Walking through the airport, she felt a wave of sonder as each face passed.
secondExample: A moment of sonder made the argument feel suddenly small.
useCase: Use it when empathy arrives all at once.
origin: Coined by John Koenig for The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows, echoing French sonder, “to probe the depths.”

shortDefinition: A deep, bittersweet longing for something absent.
longDefinition: A tender ache for a person, place, or time that is gone — melancholy and warm at once.
example: The old song filled him with saudade for a city he had left long ago.
secondExample: There is saudade in every goodbye that goes well.
useCase: Use it for love and loss felt together.
origin: Portuguese, traced to Latin solitas, “solitude” — famously hard to translate.

shortDefinition: The earthy smell after rain on dry ground.
longDefinition: The clean, mineral scent that rises when rain first touches warm, dry earth.
example: She opened the window for the petrichor after the storm.
secondExample: Petrichor is the smell of relief.
useCase: Use it for the scent of the first rain.
origin: Coined in 1964 from Greek petra, “stone,” and ichor, the fluid in the veins of the gods.

shortDefinition: Twilight; the soft light after sunset.
longDefinition: The dim, glowing hour between sunset and dark, when the day quietly lets go.
example: They walked home through the gloaming.
secondExample: In the gloaming the hills turned the color of smoke.
useCase: Use it for the gentle light of dusk.
origin: From Old English glōmung, related to glōm, “twilight,” and glow.

shortDefinition: Calm, peaceful, and happy — often of a remembered time.
longDefinition: Idyllically tranquil; especially used of golden days looked back on with fondness.
example: They spoke of the halcyon days before the move.
secondExample: The lake was halcyon at dawn.
useCase: Use it for serene, golden calm.
origin: From Greek halkyōn, the kingfisher, mythically said to calm the seas while nesting.

shortDefinition: A soft whispering or rustling sound.
longDefinition: A low, continuous murmur — wind in leaves, distant voices, the hush of a crowd.
example: A susurrus of leaves followed them down the lane.
secondExample: The library held a faint susurrus of turning pages.
useCase: Use it for gentle, layered whispering sound.
origin: From Latin susurrus, “a humming, whispering.”

shortDefinition: Lasting only a very short time.
longDefinition: Fleeting and short-lived — beautiful partly because it cannot stay.
example: Cherry blossoms are loved for being ephemeral.
secondExample: Fame can be ephemeral; character lasts.
useCase: Use it for the brief and passing.
origin: From Greek ephēmeros, “lasting only a day,” from epi-, “on,” + hēmera, “day.”

shortDefinition: Filled with a sense of the sacred or awe-inspiring.
longDefinition: Suggesting the presence of something greater than ourselves — mysterious and elevated.
example: The cathedral had a numinous stillness.
secondExample: There was something numinous about the silent forest.
useCase: Use it for awe that feels close to the sacred.
origin: From Latin numen, “divine will, a nod of the gods.”

shortDefinition: Full of or giving off light.
longDefinition: Glowing, radiant, bright — also used for ideas or faces that seem to shine.
example: The snow was luminous under the full moon.
secondExample: She had a luminous, easy smile.
useCase: Use it for radiant brightness, literal or felt.
origin: From Latin lumen, “light.”

shortDefinition: Finding something good without looking for it.
longDefinition: A happy accident — the knack of stumbling onto fortune while searching for something else.
example: Meeting her was pure serendipity.
secondExample: The best discoveries often come from serendipity.
useCase: Use it for lucky, unplanned good fortune.
origin: Coined in 1754 by Horace Walpole, from the Persian tale “The Three Princes of Serendip.”

longDefinition: Flowing pleasantly, used especially of voices, music, or language.
example: His mellifluous voice made the reading feel intimate.
secondExample: The sentence was too mellifluous to cut.
useCase: Use it for beautiful, flowing sound.
origin: From Latin mellifluus: mel, “honey,” + fluere, “to flow.”

longDefinition: The ability to stay balanced and composed when circumstances turn difficult.
example: He answered the criticism with surprising equanimity.
secondExample: Equanimity is not coldness; it is steadiness.
useCase: Use it for poise that holds under pressure.
origin: From Latin aequanimitas: aequus, “even,” + animus, “mind.”

shortDefinition: Calmly wise and quick to see what matters.
longDefinition: Showing practical wisdom and sharp judgment before anyone else does.
example: Her sagacious advice cut through the panic without sounding cold.
secondExample: A sagacious leader knows exactly when not to react.
useCase: Use it for quietly excellent judgment.
origin: From Latin sagax, “keen-scented, perceptive.”

longDefinition: Noble-minded, especially toward someone with less power or who has erred.
example: Her magnanimous response ended the conflict quickly.
secondExample: A magnanimous winner never humiliates the loser.
useCase: Use it for big-hearted generosity.
origin: From Latin magnanimus: magnus, “great,” + animus, “soul.”

shortDefinition: Deeply touching, often with a trace of sadness.
longDefinition: Emotionally piercing because something feels tender, beautiful, or quietly painful.
example: The final line was poignant without being sentimental.
secondExample: It was a poignant reminder of how quickly things change.
useCase: Use it for tender emotional impact.
origin: From Old French poignant, “pricking,” from Latin pungere, “to prick.”

longDefinition: A dreamy, absorbed state where the mind wanders freely and happily.
example: The long train ride slipped into reverie.
secondExample: He broke from his reverie when the phone buzzed.
useCase: Use it for soft, wandering thought.
origin: From French rêverie, from rêver, “to dream.”

longDefinition: A soft heaviness of body or mood — slow, warm, and almost luxurious.
example: Sunday afternoon settled over the apartment with a quiet languor.
secondExample: The summer heat gave the whole city a strange languor.
useCase: Use it for slow, atmospheric tiredness.
origin: From Latin languor, “faintness,” from languere, “to be weak.”

shortDefinition: Existing on a threshold, between two states.
longDefinition: Belonging to an in-between moment — no longer one thing, not yet the next.
example: Graduation felt liminal: no longer a student, not yet anything else.
secondExample: Empty airports at night have a liminal quality.
useCase: Use it for thresholds and transitions.
origin: From Latin limen, “threshold.”

longDefinition: Implied through behavior, silence, or context rather than stated out loud.
example: Their tacit agreement kept the meeting moving.
secondExample: There was a tacit expectation that everyone would stay late.
useCase: Use it for unspoken understanding.
origin: From Latin tacitus, “silent,” from tacere, “to be silent.”

longDefinition: Expressed with cutting precision, especially in criticism or analysis.
example: Her trenchant comment changed the direction of the debate.
secondExample: A trenchant edit can rescue a weak paragraph.
useCase: Use it for sharp insight that cuts to the point.
origin: From Old French trenchant, “cutting,” from trenchier, “to cut.”

longDefinition: Concise but forceful — saying a great deal in very few words.
example: His pithy summary said more than the whole report.
secondExample: She has a gift for pithy advice.
useCase: Use it for short lines that carry weight.
origin: From Old English piþa, “pith,” the dense core of a plant stem.

shortDefinition: Amusing in a quiet, odd way.
longDefinition: Funny through dryness or quirk rather than loud jokes.
example: He made a droll remark and went back to his coffee.
secondExample: Her droll expression was funnier than anything she said.
useCase: Use it for dry, understated humor.
origin: From French drôle, “buffoon, rascal.”

longDefinition: A sharp retort that answers a remark with wit — originally a fencing counter-thrust.
example: Her riposte drew a laugh from the whole table.
secondExample: He had no riposte ready, so he simply smiled.
useCase: Use it for a fast, witty comeback.
origin: From French riposte, from Italian risposta, “answer” — a fencer’s return hit.

longDefinition: Relaxed and unworried — sometimes charmingly carefree, sometimes carelessly so.
example: His insouciant reply made everyone else feel less anxious.
secondExample: She walked in with an insouciant ease.
useCase: Use it for effortless nonchalance.
origin: From French insouciant: in-, “not,” + souci, “care.”

longDefinition: Self-possessed assurance, especially in a difficult or public moment.
example: She handled the tense question with aplomb.
secondExample: Aplomb makes confidence look effortless.
useCase: Use it for graceful confidence under pressure.
origin: From French aplomb, “poise,” literally à plomb, “according to the plumb line.”

shortDefinition: Very attentive to detail; hard to please.
longDefinition: Careful and exacting, with standards high enough that small flaws rarely slip past.
example: Her fastidious notes caught errors everyone else missed.
secondExample: He was fastidious about the rhythm of each sentence.
useCase: Use it for precise, exacting standards.
origin: From Latin fastidium, “loathing, squeamishness.”

longDefinition: Careful perception that separates what is true, useful, or important from what only seems so.
example: Good taste begins with discernment.
secondExample: Discernment helped her ignore the urgent-but-unimportant.
useCase: Use it for refined, careful judgment.
origin: From Latin discernere, “to separate, distinguish.”

longDefinition: A willing eagerness to act — doing something promptly and gladly.
example: She accepted the challenge with alacrity.
secondExample: His alacrity made the whole project feel lighter.
useCase: Use it for eager, energetic willingness.
origin: From Latin alacritas, “liveliness,” from alacer, “lively.”

longDefinition: Showing steady diligence and persistence over time, not just in bursts.
example: Her assiduous practice made progress almost inevitable.
secondExample: Assiduous readers notice what others skim past.
useCase: Use it for disciplined consistency.
origin: From Latin assiduus, “constant,” from assidere, “to sit beside.”

longDefinition: Persuasive because the reasoning is strong and easy to follow.
example: He made a cogent case for changing the plan.
secondExample: A cogent paragraph needs no decoration.
useCase: Use it for persuasive, well-built reasoning.
origin: From Latin cogere, “to compel,” from co- + agere, “to drive.”

longDefinition: An animated brightness of spirit, expression, or personality.
example: Her vivacity changed the mood of the room.
secondExample: The essay had real intellectual vivacity.
useCase: Use it for lively, infectious brightness.
origin: From Latin vivacitas, from vivax, “lively,” from vivere, “to live.”

longDefinition: Plain, everyday, or lacking poetic color and originality.
example: The explanation was accurate but prosaic.
secondExample: He turned a prosaic errand into a small adventure.
useCase: Use it for plainness that feels uninspired.
origin: From Latin prosaicus, “in prose,” from prosa, “prose.”

shortDefinition: A strong desire to travel and explore.
longDefinition: An itch to leave the familiar behind and see somewhere new.
example: Spring always stirs her wanderlust.
secondExample: A single photograph was enough to spark his wanderlust.
useCase: Use it for the pull toward the road.
origin: From German Wandern, “to hike,” + Lust, “desire.”

longDefinition: A temporary visit or residence, usually away from home.
example: Their sojourn by the coast lasted a single, perfect week.
secondExample: After a brief sojourn abroad, she came home changed.
useCase: Use it for a brief, intentional stay.
origin: From Old French sojorner, from Late Latin subdiurnare, “to spend the day.”

shortDefinition: The giddy, obsessive early rush of infatuation.
longDefinition: An involuntary state of intense romantic longing — intrusive thoughts and the ache to be wanted back.
example: What he called love was mostly limerence, bright and unsteady.
secondExample: Limerence fades; affection is what stays.
useCase: Use it for the heady first stage of a crush.
origin: Coined in 1979 by psychologist Dorothy Tennov, with no deeper root.

longDefinition: Goodwill and harmony between people or groups.
example: Years of amity made the partnership easy.
secondExample: They parted in amity rather than anger.
useCase: Use it for warm, settled friendliness.
origin: From Latin amicitia, from amicus, “friend.”

shortDefinition: A longing for a home you can’t return to.
longDefinition: A Welsh ache for a place, time, or way of life that may never have fully existed.
example: Hearing the old language gave her a quiet hiraeth.
secondExample: Hiraeth is missing a home that has changed beyond returning.
useCase: Use it for homesickness with no home to go back to.
origin: Welsh, with no exact English equivalent — part grief, part belonging.

shortDefinition: The warmth of the sun in winter.
longDefinition: The specific comfort of sunlight felt on a cold day — a rare old word for a small, real pleasure.
example: They sat on the steps for a little apricity before the wind returned.
secondExample: Winter has its own gift: the apricity of a clear noon.
useCase: Use it for cold-weather sunshine on the skin.
origin: From Latin apricitas, from apricus, “exposed to the sun.”

shortDefinition: The scent that lingers after someone passes.
longDefinition: The trail a perfume leaves in the air — the fragrant wake of a person who has just left.
example: Her sillage stayed in the hallway long after she’d gone.
secondExample: He knew she’d been there from the sillage alone.
useCase: Use it for a lingering trail of scent.
origin: From French sillage, “wake,” the trail a ship leaves on water.

shortDefinition: A natural talent for doing something well.
longDefinition: An easy skill or trick that seems to come without much effort.
example: She has a knack for calming nervous people.
secondExample: There’s a knack to folding a fitted sheet.
useCase: Use it for an effortless, almost intuitive skill.
origin: From Middle English knak, “a sharp blow or trick,” of imitative origin.

longDefinition: The central idea or essence, stripped of the details.
example: I missed the start, but I got the gist.
secondExample: Give me the gist before the full report.
useCase: Use it for the core takeaway in a nutshell.
origin: From Anglo-French gist, “it lies,” the point on which a matter rests.

shortDefinition: The most important or decisive point.
longDefinition: The central difficulty or key part on which everything turns.
example: The crux of the issue is trust, not money.
secondExample: Here’s the crux: we can’t do both.
useCase: Use it for the pivotal heart of a problem.
origin: From Latin crux, “cross” — a puzzle that torments like a crossroads.

longDefinition: Something that happens by chance rather than skill or plan.
example: Winning twice was no fluke — she practiced for years.
secondExample: By a fluke, the two emails crossed.
useCase: Use it for a chance result, good or odd.
origin: Originally a lucky shot in billiards; of uncertain 19th-century origin.

shortDefinition: A feeling about something you can’t fully explain.
longDefinition: An intuitive guess that arrives before the evidence does.
example: I had a hunch the meeting would be cancelled.
secondExample: Go with your hunch on this one.
useCase: Use it for a gut feeling that proves useful.
origin: From the idea of giving a “hunch” (nudge) — slang for an inside tip.

shortDefinition: A sudden, passing wish or impulse.
longDefinition: A spontaneous desire that comes without reason and often fades fast.
example: On a whim, they drove to the coast.
secondExample: He changed the plan on a whim.
useCase: Use it for a spur-of-the-moment impulse.
origin: Shortened from whim-wham, “a fanciful object,” of obscure origin.

shortDefinition: Room to act freely; margin to maneuver.
longDefinition: Extra space, time, or freedom that lets you adjust without trouble.
example: The deadline gives us a little leeway.
secondExample: New managers deserve some leeway at first.
useCase: Use it for breathing room or flexibility.
origin: A sailing term: the sideways drift of a ship to leeward, away from the wind.

shortDefinition: Done without preparation; on the spot.
longDefinition: Made or performed spontaneously, with no rehearsal or plan.
example: They gave an impromptu speech at dinner.
secondExample: An impromptu picnic turned into the best day.
useCase: Use it for something delightfully unplanned.
origin: From Latin in promptu, “in readiness,” via French.

shortDefinition: Ordinary, everyday, and a little dull.
longDefinition: Concerned with the routine and commonplace rather than the exciting.
example: Even mundane chores feel better with music.
secondExample: She finds poetry in mundane details.
useCase: Use it for the plain stuff of daily life.
origin: From Latin mundanus, “of the world,” from mundus, “world.”

longDefinition: Economical and thrifty, avoiding waste without being mean.
example: Their frugal habits added up to a real cushion.
secondExample: A frugal meal can still be delicious.
useCase: Use it for wise, careful spending.
origin: From Latin frugalis, “useful, thrifty,” from frux, “fruit, value.”

longDefinition: So central that everything else depends on or turns around it.
example: That hire proved pivotal to the company’s rise.
secondExample: We’re at a pivotal moment for the project.
useCase: Use it for a make-or-break turning point.
origin: From pivot, via French pivot, “a pin on which something turns.”

shortDefinition: The power to influence or get more from less.
longDefinition: An advantage that multiplies your effort or strengthens your position.
example: Owning the patent gave them real leverage.
secondExample: Use your strengths as leverage in the negotiation.
useCase: Use it for an advantage that amplifies effort.
origin: From lever, via Old French levier, from Latin levare, “to raise.”

shortDefinition: The force of something already in motion.
longDefinition: The driving energy that keeps progress going once it’s started.
example: A few quick wins built real momentum.
secondExample: Don’t lose momentum now that it’s working.
useCase: Use it for progress that feeds on itself.
origin: From Latin momentum, “movement, moving power,” from movere, “to move.”

shortDefinition: To make something simpler and more efficient.
longDefinition: To remove waste or friction so a process flows smoothly.
example: They streamlined checkout from five steps to one.
secondExample: We need to streamline how we onboard new hires.
useCase: Use it for cutting friction and clutter.
origin: From the smooth shape that lets air or water flow past with least resistance.

longDefinition: Showing good judgment and caution, especially about consequences.
example: It’s prudent to keep a few months’ savings.
secondExample: A prudent driver leaves room to brake.
useCase: Use it for sensible, forward-looking caution.
origin: From Latin prudens, a contraction of providens, “foreseeing.”

longDefinition: Able to work, survive, or be done in practice.
example: It’s the only viable option on the table.
secondExample: Is the business model actually viable?
useCase: Use it for something realistically doable.
origin: From French viable, “capable of living,” from vie, “life.”

longDefinition: Expert and proficient through practice or natural ability.
example: She’s adept at turning chaos into a plan.
secondExample: He became adept at reading a room.
useCase: Use it for polished, capable skill.
origin: From Latin adeptus, “having attained,” from adipisci, “to achieve.”

longDefinition: An immediate sense of what’s true or right that bypasses step-by-step logic.
example: Her intuition about people is rarely wrong.
secondExample: Trust your intuition, then check it.
useCase: Use it for direct, instinctive knowing.
origin: From Latin intueri, “to look at, contemplate.”

longDefinition: A moment when something becomes clear all at once, often life-changing.
example: She had an epiphany in the shower and rewrote the whole plan.
secondExample: It wasn’t logic; it was an epiphany.
useCase: Use it for a flash of sudden understanding.
origin: From Greek epiphaneia, “manifestation, appearing.”

shortDefinition: Looking inward at your own thoughts and feelings.
longDefinition: The examination of one’s own mental and emotional state.
example: A little introspection showed him what really bothered him.
secondExample: The trip became a time of quiet introspection.
useCase: Use it for honest inner reflection.
origin: From Latin introspicere, “to look inside,” from intro- + specere, “to look.”

shortDefinition: Inclined to question and not take on faith.
longDefinition: Doubtful by habit, wanting evidence before believing a claim.
example: Be skeptical of headlines that sound too neat.
secondExample: She was skeptical until she saw the data.
useCase: Use it for healthy, questioning doubt.
origin: From Greek skeptikos, “inquiring, thoughtful,” from skeptesthai, “to examine.”

shortDefinition: To work something out from clues.
longDefinition: To reach a conclusion from evidence and reasoning rather than direct statement.
example: From her tone, I inferred she disagreed.
secondExample: We can infer the cause from the pattern.
useCase: Use it for reasoning to a conclusion.
origin: From Latin inferre, “to bring in, deduce,” from in- + ferre, “to carry.”

shortDefinition: To examine very closely and critically.
longDefinition: To inspect something in careful detail, looking for flaws or truth.
example: Auditors scrutinized every line of the budget.
secondExample: She scrutinized the contract before signing.
useCase: Use it for close, searching examination.
origin: From Latin scrutari, “to search,” originally “to sort through rubbish.”

shortDefinition: Very deep in meaning or feeling.
longDefinition: Reaching far below the surface — intellectually deep or intensely felt.
example: It was a profound book that changed how she thought.
secondExample: There was a profound silence after the news.
useCase: Use it for depth that stays with you.
origin: From Latin profundus, “deep,” from pro- + fundus, “bottom.”

shortDefinition: The art of persuasive speaking or writing.
longDefinition: Language designed to influence — sometimes powerfully, sometimes emptily.
example: Strip away the rhetoric and the plan is thin.
secondExample: She studied the rhetoric of great speeches.
useCase: Use it for persuasive (or empty) language.
origin: From Greek rhētorikē, “art of the orator,” from rhētōr, “speaker.”

shortDefinition: Good at making people agree or act.
longDefinition: Able to convince through reasoning, charm, or evidence.
example: She made a persuasive case for going early.
secondExample: His most persuasive argument was simply showing up.
useCase: Use it for convincing, winning over others.
origin: From Latin persuadere, “to convince,” from per- + suadere, “to advise.”

longDefinition: Tactful and careful in dealing with others, avoiding offense.
example: She gave a diplomatic answer that pleased both sides.
secondExample: It took a diplomatic touch to settle the feud.
useCase: Use it for tactful, even-handed handling.
origin: From Greek diplōma, “folded document,” the credential of an envoy.

shortDefinition: Direct and plain-spoken, sometimes too much.
longDefinition: Saying things straight, without softening, even at the risk of bruising.
example: To be blunt, the draft isn’t ready.
secondExample: His blunt feedback was hard but useful.
useCase: Use it for unvarnished directness.
origin: From Middle English blunt, “dull, not sharp” — a dull edge that doesn’t cut gently.

shortDefinition: Using far more words than needed.
longDefinition: Wordy to the point of burying the point.
example: The contract was so verbose no one read it.
secondExample: Cut the verbose intro and start with the news.
useCase: Use it for long-winded, padded language.
origin: From Latin verbosus, “full of words,” from verbum, “word.”

shortDefinition: A hint, usually suggestive or unkind.
longDefinition: An indirect remark that implies something without saying it outright.
example: The article was full of innuendo and short on facts.
secondExample: He answered the innuendo with a flat denial.
useCase: Use it for sly, implied suggestion.
origin: From Latin innuendo, “by nodding to,” from innuere, “to hint.”

shortDefinition: Spotlessly clean, as if brand new.
longDefinition: In perfect, unspoiled condition — fresh, pure, untouched.
example: The cabin sat beside a pristine lake.
secondExample: He keeps his records in pristine condition.
useCase: Use it for the untouched and immaculate.
origin: From Latin pristinus, “former, original,” the pure first state.

longDefinition: So airy, fine, or beautiful it seems to belong to another, lighter world.
example: The fog gave the valley an ethereal glow.
secondExample: Her voice was ethereal, barely there.
useCase: Use it for delicate, heavenly lightness.
origin: From Greek aithēr, “upper air,” the pure air the gods breathed.

shortDefinition: Inactive for now, but able to wake.
longDefinition: Temporarily at rest or asleep, with the potential to become active again.
example: The volcano has been dormant for centuries.
secondExample: A dormant talent woke up in his forties.
useCase: Use it for sleeping potential, not gone.
origin: From French dormir, “to sleep,” from Latin dormire.

longDefinition: Belonging to or happening in the night, like owls and night owls alike.
example: Owls are nocturnal hunters.
secondExample: She’s nocturnal — her best ideas come at 2 a.m.
useCase: Use it for creatures and habits of the night.
origin: From Latin nocturnalis, from nox, “night.”

shortDefinition: A stretch of land and its features.
longDefinition: The physical character of an area of ground; also a tricky area to navigate.
example: The terrain turned rocky past the ridge.
secondExample: Legal terrain like this needs an expert.
useCase: Use it for landscape, literal or figurative.
origin: From French terrain, from Latin terrenum, “land,” from terra, “earth.”

shortDefinition: The natural home of a plant or animal.
longDefinition: The environment where a species naturally lives and thrives.
example: Wetlands are a vital habitat for migrating birds.
secondExample: The café is his natural habitat.
useCase: Use it for where something belongs and flourishes.
origin: From Latin habitat, “it dwells,” from habitare, “to live in.”

shortDefinition: To be drawn naturally toward something.
longDefinition: To move toward a person, place, or thing as if pulled by gravity.
example: Guests always gravitate to the kitchen.
secondExample: She gravitates toward stories about the sea.
useCase: Use it for a natural, magnetic pull.
origin: From Latin gravitas, “weight,” the same root as gravity.

shortDefinition: The accepted rules of polite behavior.
longDefinition: The customs that govern how people are expected to behave in a group.
example: Email etiquette varies a lot between companies.
secondExample: There’s a quiet etiquette to a shared kitchen.
useCase: Use it for the unwritten rules of conduct.
origin: From French étiquette, “label, ticket” — the court rules posted on a card.

longDefinition: A subject or act banned or avoided because a culture treats it as off-limits.
example: Money used to be a taboo at the dinner table.
secondExample: The film broke a long-standing taboo.
useCase: Use it for a culturally forbidden subject.
origin: From Tongan tabu, “forbidden, sacred,” recorded by Captain Cook in 1777.

shortDefinition: At home anywhere; worldly and diverse.
longDefinition: Familiar with and comfortable among many cultures and places.
example: She has a cosmopolitan ease in any city.
secondExample: It’s a small but cosmopolitan town.
useCase: Use it for a worldly, well-traveled sensibility.
origin: From Greek kosmopolitēs, “citizen of the world,” kosmos + politēs.

shortDefinition: The guiding spirit or values of a group.
longDefinition: The characteristic beliefs and attitudes that define a community or person.
example: Generosity is part of the company’s ethos.
secondExample: The whole town shares a quiet, hardy ethos.
useCase: Use it for a defining set of values.
origin: From Greek ēthos, “character, custom.”

shortDefinition: Boldly new and ahead of its time.
longDefinition: Experimental and innovative, especially in art and ideas, before the mainstream catches up.
example: Her avant-garde designs baffled critics, then defined the decade.
secondExample: The gallery favors avant-garde work.
useCase: Use it for the daring cutting edge.
origin: From French avant-garde, “advance guard,” the troops who go first.

shortDefinition: Warm friendship and trust within a group.
longDefinition: The easy loyalty and good feeling shared by people who do things together.
example: The long hours built real camaraderie on the crew.
secondExample: There’s a camaraderie among early risers.
useCase: Use it for team spirit and shared warmth.
origin: From French camaraderie, from camarade, “comrade,” from Latin camera, “room.”

shortDefinition: A magnetic charm that draws people in.
longDefinition: A compelling presence that inspires attention, affection, or loyalty.
example: Her charisma filled the room before she spoke.
secondExample: Charisma can’t be faked for long.
useCase: Use it for magnetic personal appeal.
origin: From Greek kharisma, “gift, grace,” from kharis, “favor.”

longDefinition: The quality of being whole and consistent in your values, even when no one’s watching.
example: He had the integrity to admit his own mistake.
secondExample: Cutting that corner would cost us our integrity.
useCase: Use it for unbending honesty and wholeness.
origin: From Latin integritas, “wholeness, soundness,” from integer, “whole.”

longDefinition: Enduring pain or difficulty without showing feeling or asking for sympathy.
example: She stayed stoic through the whole ordeal.
secondExample: His stoic shrug hid a lot of worry.
useCase: Use it for quiet, unflinching endurance.
origin: From the Stoics, Greek philosophers who taught calm acceptance, named for the stoa (porch) where they met.

longDefinition: Willing to take surprising risks or break with caution and convention.
example: It was an audacious plan, and it worked.
secondExample: She made an audacious career leap at fifty.
useCase: Use it for fearless, eyebrow-raising boldness.
origin: From Latin audacia, “boldness,” from audere, “to dare.”

shortDefinition: To keep putting off what you should do.
longDefinition: To delay a task, usually one that feels hard or dull, until later.
example: He procrastinated until the night before.
secondExample: We procrastinate most on the things we care about.
useCase: Use it for delaying what you ought to start.
origin: From Latin procrastinare, “to put off until tomorrow,” from cras, “tomorrow.”

longDefinition: To grow smaller or less, little by little.
example: Their savings dwindled over the long winter.
secondExample: The crowd dwindled to a handful.
useCase: Use it for a slow, steady shrinking.
origin: From Old English dwinan, “to waste away.”

shortDefinition: To stay a little longer than needed.
longDefinition: To remain, slow to leave, or to persist faintly afterward.
example: They lingered at the table long after dessert.
secondExample: The smell of coffee lingered all morning.
useCase: Use it for staying, or a trace that won’t leave.
origin: From Old English lengan, “to prolong,” related to long.

shortDefinition: Quick, energetic, and a little sharp.
longDefinition: Lively and efficient in pace, or fresh and bracing like cold air.
example: They went for a brisk walk before breakfast.
secondExample: She gave a brisk, friendly nod and moved on.
useCase: Use it for a fresh, energetic briskness.
origin: Probably from French brusque, “lively, rough.”

longDefinition: Heavy, unwieldy, or overly complicated to use or manage.
example: The old process was cumbersome and slow.
secondExample: That’s a cumbersome suitcase for a weekend.
useCase: Use it for the clunky and unwieldy.
origin: From cumber, “to hinder,” from Old French combrer, “to block.”

shortDefinition: To fiddle and adjust, often just for fun.
longDefinition: To make small changes to something, experimenting rather than overhauling.
example: He likes to tinker with old radios on weekends.
secondExample: Stop tinkering — it already works.
useCase: Use it for playful, hands-on adjusting.
origin: From the medieval tinker, a traveling mender of pots and pans.

shortDefinition: To wander slowly with no direct route.
longDefinition: To follow a winding, unhurried path — a river, a walk, or a conversation.
example: The path meanders along the river.
secondExample: His story meandered before reaching the point.
useCase: Use it for a pleasant, aimless wander.
origin: From the Maeander, a famously winding river in ancient Turkey.

shortDefinition: Done freely on impulse, without planning.
longDefinition: Arising naturally from the moment rather than from a plan.
example: A spontaneous round of applause broke out.
secondExample: She’s spontaneous — say the word and she’s packed.
useCase: Use it for the unplanned and natural.
origin: From Latin spontaneus, “of one’s free will,” from sponte, “willingly.”

shortDefinition: To make it up as you go.
longDefinition: To create or perform without preparation, adapting on the spot.
example: The speaker lost her notes and improvised brilliantly.
secondExample: We had no recipe, so we improvised.
useCase: Use it for inventing in the moment.
origin: From Latin improvisus, “unforeseen,” from in- + providere, “to foresee.”

longDefinition: Showing generosity and good manners, especially toward others.
example: She was gracious even in defeat.
secondExample: A gracious host makes everyone feel chosen.
useCase: Use it for warm, generous courtesy.
origin: From Latin gratiosus, “pleasing, agreeable,” from gratia, “grace.”

longDefinition: Showing deep, genuine conviction rather than joking or half-heartedness.
example: He made an earnest effort to make things right.
secondExample: Her earnest face told us she meant it.
useCase: Use it for heartfelt, genuine seriousness.
origin: From Old English eornost, “seriousness, zeal.”

shortDefinition: Kind and wishing good for others.
longDefinition: Generous and well-meaning, inclined to help rather than harm.
example: A benevolent stranger paid for their meal.
secondExample: The fund had a benevolent purpose.
useCase: Use it for active, good-willed kindness.
origin: From Latin benevolens, “well-wishing,” bene, “well,” + velle, “to wish.”

longDefinition: Believing that others act mainly out of self-interest, and quick to doubt sincerity.
example: Years in the business left him cynical.
secondExample: Don’t be so cynical — some people just are kind.
useCase: Use it for jaded, suspicious doubt.
origin: From Greek kynikos, “dog-like,” the nickname of blunt ancient philosophers.

longDefinition: Showing casual calm, as if nothing could ruffle you.
example: She gave a nonchalant shrug at the bad news.
secondExample: He stayed nonchalant even as the deadline loomed.
useCase: Use it for studied, easy indifference.
origin: From French nonchalant, “not caring,” from non- + chaloir, “to matter.”

shortDefinition: Mysterious and hard to figure out.
longDefinition: Puzzling or ambiguous in a way that invites curiosity.
example: She gave an enigmatic smile and said nothing.
secondExample: The painting’s enigmatic mood drew crowds.
useCase: Use it for the intriguingly mysterious.
origin: From Greek ainigma, “riddle,” from ainissesthai, “to speak in riddles.”

longDefinition: Profitable; producing a good financial return.
example: Consulting turned out to be surprisingly lucrative.
secondExample: It’s a lucrative niche if you can crack it.
useCase: Use it for the genuinely profitable.
origin: From Latin lucrativus, “gainful,” from lucrum, “profit.”

shortDefinition: The drive to act without being told.
longDefinition: The readiness to start things on your own and take charge.
example: She showed real initiative by fixing it herself.
secondExample: Take the initiative — don’t wait for permission.
useCase: Use it for self-starting drive.
origin: From Latin initiare, “to begin,” from initium, “beginning.”

shortDefinition: To hand a task to someone else.
longDefinition: To entrust work or authority to another person so you can focus elsewhere.
example: Good leaders delegate instead of doing it all.
secondExample: Learn to delegate before you burn out.
useCase: Use it for sharing the load wisely.
origin: From Latin delegare, “to send on a commission,” from de- + legare, “to depute.”

shortDefinition: Acting ahead of a problem, not after.
longDefinition: Taking initiative to shape events rather than just reacting to them.
example: A proactive fix saved them a week of cleanup.
secondExample: Be proactive: flag risks before they bite.
useCase: Use it for getting ahead of trouble.
origin: Coined in the 20th century from pro-, “before,” plus active.

shortDefinition: To aim high toward a goal.
longDefinition: To direct your hopes and effort toward something worth reaching for.
example: She aspires to run her own studio one day.
secondExample: We aspire to make it simple, not just powerful.
useCase: Use it for reaching toward a higher goal.
origin: From Latin aspirare, “to breathe toward,” from ad- + spirare, “to breathe.”

longDefinition: Persistently intense or harsh, refusing to soften or stop.
example: Her relentless focus finally paid off.
secondExample: The rain was relentless all weekend.
useCase: Use it for unstopping intensity.
origin: From relent, “to soften,” with -less — literally without softening.

shortDefinition: Full of initiative and bold ideas.
longDefinition: Quick to spot opportunity and resourceful enough to act on it.
example: An enterprising student turned the club into a business.
secondExample: It took an enterprising mind to see the gap.
useCase: Use it for resourceful, opportunity-seeking drive.
origin: From enterprise, from Old French entreprise, “an undertaking.”

shortDefinition: Based on clear reason, not emotion.
longDefinition: Guided by logic and sensible thinking rather than feeling or impulse.
example: Let’s make a rational decision, not a panicked one.
secondExample: There’s a rational explanation for the noise.
useCase: Use it for clear-headed, logical thinking.
origin: From Latin rationalis, “of reason,” from ratio, “reckoning, reason.”

shortDefinition: Based on facts, not personal feeling.
longDefinition: Considering things fairly and impartially, free of bias.
example: Try to stay objective when you review your own work.
secondExample: We need an objective measure, not a hunch.
useCase: Use it for fair, unbiased judgment.
origin: From Latin obiectum, “thing presented to the mind,” from obicere, “to throw before.”

shortDefinition: To think about deeply and calmly.
longDefinition: To consider something thoughtfully, or to gaze at it in reflection.
example: She sat by the window, contemplating the offer.
secondExample: He contemplated the sea for a long while.
useCase: Use it for unhurried, deep thought.
origin: From Latin contemplari, “to observe, survey,” originally a templum, a space marked for omens.

shortDefinition: To turn something over in your mind.
longDefinition: To weigh an idea or question carefully, taking your time.
example: He pondered the question before answering.
secondExample: She likes to ponder a problem on a walk.
useCase: Use it for careful, weighing thought.
origin: From Latin ponderare, “to weigh,” from pondus, “weight.”

shortDefinition: To think something over and over.
longDefinition: To turn an idea or worry repeatedly in the mind — deep reflection, sometimes the unhelpful kind.
example: He ruminated on the decision for days.
secondExample: Try not to ruminate on one comment all night.
useCase: Use it for slow, circling thought.
origin: From Latin ruminare, “to chew the cud,” as a cow re-chews its food.

longDefinition: Connected and consistent so that the parts make sense as a whole.
example: After coffee, his thoughts were finally coherent.
secondExample: We need a coherent strategy, not five half-plans.
useCase: Use it for ideas that hang together clearly.
origin: From Latin cohaerere, “to stick together,” from co- + haerere, “to cling.”

shortDefinition: Open to more than one meaning.
longDefinition: Unclear or uncertain because it can be understood in several ways.
example: The wording was ambiguous, so half of us showed up early.
secondExample: Her answer was deliberately ambiguous.
useCase: Use it for the unclear and double-meaning.
origin: From Latin ambiguus, “doubtful,” from ambigere, “to wander about.”

longDefinition: A puzzle or dilemma with no easy answer.
example: Pricing the new product was a real conundrum.
secondExample: Here’s the conundrum: both options cost us something.
useCase: Use it for a knotty, puzzling problem.
origin: Of uncertain 16th-century origin, perhaps mock-Latin university slang.

shortDefinition: A statement that contradicts itself yet rings true.
longDefinition: A situation or idea that seems self-contradictory but reveals a deeper truth.
example: It’s a paradox: to go faster, slow down.
secondExample: The paradox of choice is that more options can paralyze us.
useCase: Use it for a true-but-contradictory idea.
origin: From Greek paradoxon, “contrary to expectation,” para- + doxa, “opinion.”

longDefinition: Specialized words used by a profession or group, often baffling to everyone else.
example: Strip the jargon and just say what you mean.
secondExample: Legal jargon makes simple things sound hard.
useCase: Use it for impenetrable insider wording.
origin: From Old French jargon, “chatter, birdsong, gibberish.”

longDefinition: Informal and conversational rather than formal or literary.
example: “Gonna” is colloquial; “going to” is formal.
secondExample: The translation kept the colloquial, friendly tone.
useCase: Use it for relaxed, spoken-style language.
origin: From Latin colloquium, “conversation,” from com- + loqui, “to speak.”

shortDefinition: A gentler word for a harsh truth.
longDefinition: A mild or roundabout term used in place of one that feels blunt or unpleasant.
example: “Let go” is a euphemism for fired.
secondExample: Funerals are full of soft euphemisms.
useCase: Use it for a polite stand-in word.
origin: From Greek euphēmismos, “use of good words,” eu-, “well,” + phēmē, “speech.”

longDefinition: A brief account of a real incident, often amusing or illustrative.
example: She opened the talk with a warm anecdote.
secondExample: One anecdote isn’t proof, but it sticks.
useCase: Use it for a small, pointed story.
origin: From Greek anekdota, “things unpublished,” an- + ekdotos, “given out.”

longDefinition: Bounded in size, amount, or time — capable of running out.
example: Attention is finite, so spend it well.
secondExample: We have a finite budget and infinite ideas.
useCase: Use it for the limited and countable.
origin: From Latin finitus, “limited,” from finis, “end, boundary.”

longDefinition: Relating to the stars and sky, or seeming divinely beautiful.
example: They tracked the celestial bodies all night.
secondExample: The choir had an almost celestial sound.
useCase: Use it for the heavenly and starlit.
origin: From Latin caelestis, “heavenly,” from caelum, “sky.”

shortDefinition: Liable to change or erupt suddenly.
longDefinition: Unstable and unpredictable — a mood, a market, or a chemical ready to flare.
example: The stock has been volatile all week.
secondExample: Their relationship was passionate but volatile.
useCase: Use it for the unstable and quick to flare.
origin: From Latin volatilis, “flying, fleeting,” from volare, “to fly.”

longDefinition: The slow loss of something — rock by water and wind, or trust over time.
example: Years of erosion carved the canyon.
secondExample: Small broken promises caused a slow erosion of trust.
useCase: Use it for a slow, grinding loss.
origin: From Latin erodere, “to gnaw away,” from e- + rodere, “to gnaw.”

longDefinition: Showing luminous colors that seem to change as the light or angle moves.
example: The beetle’s shell was iridescent in the sun.
secondExample: Oil on water spreads in iridescent swirls.
useCase: Use it for shimmering, color-shifting sheen.
origin: From Latin iris, “rainbow,” the messenger goddess of the rainbow.

longDefinition: An intense, wistful desire for someone or something.
example: There was a yearning in her voice when she spoke of home.
secondExample: He felt a sudden yearning to start over.
useCase: Use it for heartfelt, aching desire.
origin: From Old English geornan, “to desire,” related to eager.

longDefinition: A peaceful satisfaction with what you have, without restless wanting.
example: There’s a deep contentment in a slow Sunday.
secondExample: He traded ambition for contentment, and didn’t regret it.
useCase: Use it for calm, sufficient happiness.
origin: From Latin contentus, “satisfied,” from continere, “to hold together.”

shortDefinition: Openness to being hurt — and to being known.
longDefinition: The state of being exposed emotionally; also the courage to let others see you.
example: Real connection takes a little vulnerability.
secondExample: She admired his vulnerability in admitting fear.
useCase: Use it for brave emotional openness.
origin: From Latin vulnerare, “to wound,” from vulnus, “wound.”

shortDefinition: Care for others’ suffering, and the wish to ease it.
longDefinition: A tender concern for someone in pain, joined with a desire to help.
example: She treated every patient with deep compassion.
secondExample: Compassion is empathy that moves to action.
useCase: Use it for caring that wants to help.
origin: From Latin compassio, “suffering with,” com- + pati, “to suffer.”

longDefinition: A trembling unease or apprehension before something uncertain.
example: She opened the results with real trepidation.
secondExample: He approached the stage with trepidation.
useCase: Use it for anxious, fluttery dread.
origin: From Latin trepidare, “to tremble,” from trepidus, “alarmed.”

longDefinition: A spontaneous attraction or sense of connection to a person, thing, or idea.
example: She felt an instant affinity with the old city.
secondExample: He has an affinity for languages.
useCase: Use it for a natural pull or kinship.
origin: From Latin affinitas, “relationship by marriage,” from ad- + finis, “border.”

shortDefinition: So grand or beautiful it inspires awe.
longDefinition: Of the highest excellence — a beauty or scale large enough to overwhelm, in the best way.
example: The view from the ridge was simply sublime.
secondExample: There’s something sublime about a storm at sea.
useCase: Use it for awe-inspiring grandeur.
origin: From Latin sublimis, “uplifted, lofty.”

shortDefinition: Glowing with intense light or feeling.
longDefinition: Shining white-hot; also used of passion or talent so strong it seems to give off light.
example: She gave an incandescent performance.
secondExample: He was incandescent with excitement.
useCase: Use it for white-hot brilliance or intensity.
origin: From Latin incandescere, “to glow,” from candere, “to shine.”

longDefinition: Daily and commonplace — the small routines and textures that make up most of a life.
example: She found a strange beauty in quotidian things: keys, kettles, light.
secondExample: The diary recorded only quotidian details, which is why it moved him.
useCase: Use it for the ordinary made worth noticing.
origin: From Latin quotidianus, “daily,” from quotidie, “every day.”

shortDefinition: A ranking of people or things by level.
longDefinition: A system in which members are arranged in grades of authority or importance.
example: The startup tried to flatten its hierarchy.
secondExample: There’s an unspoken hierarchy in every office.
useCase: Use it for ranked layers of power or order.
origin: From Greek hierarkhia, “rule of a high priest,” hieros, “sacred,” + arkhein, “to rule.”

shortDefinition: Biased information spread to push a view.
longDefinition: Material designed to persuade a population, often misleading or one-sided.
example: The posters were pure wartime propaganda.
secondExample: Learn to tell information from propaganda.
useCase: Use it for slanted, persuasive messaging.
origin: From Latin propaganda, “things to be spread,” from propagare, “to propagate.”

shortDefinition: Unconventional and free-spirited, especially in art.
longDefinition: Living outside ordinary rules, often in a creative, carefree way.
example: They lived a bohemian life of poetry and cheap wine.
secondExample: Her flat had a cheerful bohemian clutter.
useCase: Use it for artistic, rule-bending living.
origin: From French bohémien, “gypsy,” once thought to come from Bohemia.

longDefinition: Concerned with worldly rather than spiritual or sacred matters.
example: It’s a secular school with no religious classes.
secondExample: They wanted a secular ceremony.
useCase: Use it for the worldly and non-religious.
origin: From Latin saecularis, “of an age, worldly,” from saeculum, “generation, age.”

shortDefinition: The traditional stories and customs of a people.
longDefinition: The tales, beliefs, and sayings passed down through a community.
example: The river has its own local folklore.
secondExample: Much of our folklore began as a warning.
useCase: Use it for handed-down myth and custom.
origin: Coined in 1846 from folk, “people,” + lore, “knowledge.”

shortDefinition: A meaningful journey to a special place.
longDefinition: A trip taken for reverence or significance, sacred or personal.
example: Fans make a pilgrimage to the old stadium each year.
secondExample: The hike became a kind of pilgrimage for her.
useCase: Use it for a journey that means something.
origin: From Latin peregrinus, “foreigner, traveler,” from per ager, “through the fields.”

shortDefinition: Humor used to expose folly or wrongdoing.
longDefinition: Wit, irony, or exaggeration used to criticize people or society.
example: The show is sharp political satire.
secondExample: Good satire makes you laugh, then think.
useCase: Use it for mocking that carries a point.
origin: From Latin satira, “a medley,” originally a full dish of mixed fruits.

longDefinition: Favoring equal rights, status, and opportunity for all people.
example: They ran the company on egalitarian principles.
secondExample: It was an egalitarian household, chores and all.
useCase: Use it for fairness and equal standing.
origin: From French égalité, “equality,” from Latin aequalis, “equal.”

shortDefinition: So light and delicate as to be almost see-through.
longDefinition: Sheer and translucent — used of fabric, mist, or anything fine enough that light passes through.
example: Diaphanous curtains lifted in the morning air.
secondExample: A diaphanous fog hung over the fields.
useCase: Use it for the sheer and translucent.
origin: From Greek diaphanēs: dia, “through,” + phainein, “to show.”

longDefinition: Shining with rich, impressive brilliance.
example: She arrived resplendent in deep green.
secondExample: The hall was resplendent with candlelight.
useCase: Use it for glittering, gorgeous splendor.
origin: From Latin resplendere, “to shine brightly,” from re- + splendere, “to glitter.”

shortDefinition: Vanishing almost as soon as it appears.
longDefinition: Fleeting and quick to fade, like mist or a fragrance.
example: The evanescent glow lasted only a heartbeat.
secondExample: Fame is evanescent; the work remains.
useCase: Use it for the briefly, beautifully fleeting.
origin: From Latin evanescere, “to vanish,” from e- + vanescere, “to disappear.”

shortDefinition: Deep, full, and richly resonant in sound.
longDefinition: Producing a strong, resonant tone that fills a space.
example: His sonorous voice carried to the back row.
secondExample: The bell gave a single sonorous note.
useCase: Use it for a deep, ringing richness of sound.
origin: From Latin sonorus, “resounding,” from sonor, “sound.”

longDefinition: Showing great wealth, abundance, or sumptuous display.
example: The hotel lobby was opulent to the point of excess.
secondExample: An opulent feast filled the long table.
useCase: Use it for plush, abundant luxury.
origin: From Latin opulentus, “wealthy,” from ops, “power, resources.”

longDefinition: Filled with a scent, or evoking something vividly through it.
example: The kitchen was redolent of cinnamon and rain.
secondExample: The street was redolent of his childhood.
useCase: Use it for a scent that summons a memory.
origin: From Latin redolere, “to give off a smell,” from re- + olere, “to smell.”

shortDefinition: Something extremely light, thin, and delicate.
longDefinition: Fine, filmy threads like cobweb — or anything as flimsy and delicate.
example: Dew turned the gossamer to silver thread.
secondExample: Their plan was held together by gossamer.
useCase: Use it for the impossibly fine and fragile.
origin: From Middle English gossomer, “goose summer,” the warm spell when cobwebs drift.

longDefinition: Clear like still water; also used of writing or eyes that are wonderfully clear.
example: The pool was limpid down to the pebbles.
secondExample: Her prose is limpid and unhurried.
useCase: Use it for crystal, untroubled clarity.
origin: From Latin limpidus, “clear,” perhaps related to lympha, “water.”

shortDefinition: Bubbly with energy and high spirits.
longDefinition: Lively and enthusiastic; literally, giving off bubbles like a fizzy drink.
example: Her effervescent mood lifted the whole room.
secondExample: He’s effervescent the moment music starts.
useCase: Use it for fizzy, irrepressible liveliness.
origin: From Latin effervescere, “to boil up,” from ex- + fervescere, “to begin to boil.”

shortDefinition: Expressing feeling in a musical, poetic way.
longDefinition: Full of emotion and flowing beauty, as if it could be sung.
example: The memoir turns lyrical whenever she writes about the sea.
secondExample: He gave a lyrical description of the harvest.
useCase: Use it for poetic, song-like expression.
origin: From Greek lyrikos, “singing to the lyre.”

shortDefinition: A pattern of stars — or of related things.
longDefinition: A group of stars seen as a figure, or any cluster of connected elements.
example: He pointed out each constellation by name.
secondExample: A whole constellation of small problems caused the delay.
useCase: Use it for a meaningful cluster or pattern.
origin: From Latin constellatio, “set of stars,” from com- + stella, “star.”

shortDefinition: A violent storm — of weather or feeling.
longDefinition: A furious storm, or a tumult of emotion or controversy.
example: A tempest rolled in off the sea.
secondExample: The email set off a tempest in the group chat.
useCase: Use it for a fierce storm, literal or emotional.
origin: From Latin tempestas, “weather, storm,” from tempus, “time.”

shortDefinition: A tumbling fall — of water or events.
longDefinition: A small steep waterfall, or a rush of things following one after another.
example: A cascade spilled over the mossy rocks.
secondExample: One mistake set off a cascade of problems.
useCase: Use it for a tumbling chain or fall.
origin: From Italian cascata, “waterfall,” from cascare, “to fall.”

shortDefinition: A day when day and night are equal length.
longDefinition: One of two times a year when the sun crosses the equator and light and dark are balanced.
example: The spring equinox brought the first warm afternoon.
secondExample: Ancient sites were built to mark the equinox.
useCase: Use it for the balance point of the seasons.
origin: From Latin aequinoctium: aequus, “equal,” + nox, “night.”

shortDefinition: A soft glow given off without heat.
longDefinition: Light produced by chemical or biological means rather than burning — like fireflies or deep-sea creatures.
example: The waves glowed with eerie luminescence.
secondExample: Fireflies fill the field with luminescence.
useCase: Use it for a cool, living glow.
origin: From Latin lumen, “light,” with the suffix used for ongoing processes.

longDefinition: Fresh greenery; the rich green of healthy growing plants.
example: Rain brought a sudden verdure to the hills.
secondExample: The courtyard was cool with verdure.
useCase: Use it for vivid, living green.
origin: From Old French verdure, from verd, “green,” from Latin viridis.

longDefinition: Friendly and full of warm, easy good cheer.
example: Their jovial uncle told stories all night.
secondExample: He was in a jovial mood after the win.
useCase: Use it for hearty, contagious cheer.
origin: From Latin jovialis, “of Jupiter,” whose star was thought to bring joy.

longDefinition: Showing courage and gracious, chivalrous manners.
example: It was a gallant attempt, even in defeat.
secondExample: He made a gallant offer to carry her bags.
useCase: Use it for brave, courteous nobility.
origin: From Old French galant, “merry, lively,” from gale, “merriment.”

longDefinition: Showing a shy, composed modesty, sometimes only on the surface.
example: She gave a demure smile and looked away.
secondExample: His demure manner hid a fierce ambition.
useCase: Use it for soft, modest reserve.
origin: Perhaps from Old French meür, “mature, ripe,” from Latin maturus.

longDefinition: Acting without any shame or embarrassment, often defiantly so.
example: It was a brazen lie, told to his face.
secondExample: She made a brazen grab for the corner office.
useCase: Use it for bold, unashamed nerve.
origin: From Old English bræsen, “made of brass” — hard-faced as metal.

shortDefinition: Mockingly dry and a little bitter.
longDefinition: Grimly humorous, with a scornful or cynical edge.
example: He raised an eyebrow with a sardonic smile.
secondExample: Her sardonic wit could deflate any boast.
useCase: Use it for dry, biting humor.
origin: From Greek sardonios, after a Sardinian plant said to twist the face into a grim grin.

longDefinition: Reserved and unwilling to share thoughts or feelings freely.
example: He was reticent about his plans.
secondExample: She’s reticent until she trusts you.
useCase: Use it for quiet, guarded reserve.
origin: From Latin reticere, “to keep silent,” from re- + tacere, “to be silent.”

longDefinition: Keeping apart from others, cool and uninvolved.
example: He seemed aloof, but he was just shy.
secondExample: She stayed aloof from the office gossip.
useCase: Use it for cool, standoffish distance.
origin: A sailing term, “to windward,” from a- + luff, keeping a ship’s head to the wind and away.

longDefinition: Pleasant and good-natured in a way that puts people at ease.
example: Their genial host kept everyone laughing.
secondExample: He gave a genial wave from across the street.
useCase: Use it for easy, good-natured warmth.
origin: From Latin genialis, “festive, pleasant,” from genius, “guardian spirit.”

longDefinition: Showing original, resourceful cleverness in solving a problem.
example: She found an ingenious way to save the data.
secondExample: It’s an ingenious little gadget.
useCase: Use it for inventive, clever solutions.
origin: From Latin ingeniosus, “clever, talented,” from ingenium, “natural ability.”

shortDefinition: Orderly and systematic, step by step.
longDefinition: Done in a careful, logical sequence rather than at random.
example: Her methodical search turned up the missing file.
secondExample: He’s methodical: checklist first, then work.
useCase: Use it for orderly, systematic effort.
origin: From Greek methodos, “pursuit of knowledge,” meta- + hodos, “way.”

shortDefinition: Seeing the whole, not just the parts.
longDefinition: Considering something as a complete, connected system rather than separate pieces.
example: They took a holistic view of the patient’s health.
secondExample: A holistic plan looks at people, not just numbers.
useCase: Use it for whole-system thinking.
origin: From Greek holos, “whole,” coined as “holism” in the 1920s.

shortDefinition: When the whole beats the sum of its parts.
longDefinition: Combined action that produces more than the separate efforts could alone.
example: There was real synergy between the two teams.
secondExample: Good synergy turns a group into a unit.
useCase: Use it for cooperation that multiplies results.
origin: From Greek synergia, “working together,” syn- + ergon, “work.”

shortDefinition: Showing subtle shades and fine distinctions.
longDefinition: Sensitive to small but meaningful differences rather than blunt or black-and-white.
example: She gave a nuanced take on a messy issue.
secondExample: The argument is more nuanced than the headline.
useCase: Use it for subtle, finely-shaded thinking.
origin: From nuance, from French nuance, “shade of color.”

longDefinition: Wholehearted dedication to a person, cause, or practice.
example: Her devotion to her students never wavered.
secondExample: They cared for the garden with quiet devotion.
useCase: Use it for loyal, loving commitment.
origin: From Latin devovere, “to vow,” from de- + vovere, “to vow.”

longDefinition: Softness of feeling and touch toward someone you care for.
example: He spoke of his grandmother with great tenderness.
secondExample: There was tenderness in the way she tucked them in.
useCase: Use it for soft, loving gentleness.
origin: From Latin tener, “soft, delicate.”

longDefinition: An overwhelming feeling of reverence before something vast or beautiful.
example: They stood in awe beneath the redwoods.
secondExample: The night sky still fills him with awe.
useCase: Use it for humbling, reverent wonder.
origin: From Old Norse agi, “fright, terror,” softened over time to reverence.

longDefinition: Terse and sparing in speech, often to dry or pointed effect.
example: His laconic “fine” said plenty.
secondExample: She gave a laconic reply and hung up.
useCase: Use it for terse, economical speech.
origin: From Greek Lakōnikos, “of Laconia (Sparta),” whose people were famously terse.

longDefinition: Excessively chatty, given to rambling on at length.
example: The garrulous cab driver narrated the whole route.
secondExample: He grows garrulous after one cup of coffee.
useCase: Use it for endless, chatty talkativeness.
origin: From Latin garrulus, “chattering,” from garrire, “to chatter.”

shortDefinition: Brief and to the point, sometimes curtly.
longDefinition: Sparing with words in a clean way, or short to the edge of rude.
example: Her terse reply ended the thread.
secondExample: He left a terse note: “Gone fishing.”
useCase: Use it for clipped, no-frills brevity.
origin: From Latin tersus, “wiped clean, polished,” from tergere, “to wipe.”

longDefinition: Filled with too much stuff, leaving little order or space.
example: His desk was so cluttered he lost the keys on it.
secondExample: A cluttered slide loses the room.
useCase: Use it for messy, overcrowded spaces.
origin: From clutter, a variant of clotter, “to clot or heap together.”

longDefinition: Put together quickly from whatever’s available, as a stopgap.
example: They built a makeshift shelter from branches.
secondExample: A book became a makeshift doorstop.
useCase: Use it for a quick, improvised fix.
origin: From make + shift, “to manage,” as in “make shift with what you have.”

longDefinition: Acting or flowing slowly, without the usual liveliness or speed.
example: Traffic was sluggish all morning.
secondExample: I feel sluggish after a heavy lunch.
useCase: Use it for slow, low-energy heaviness.
origin: From slug, the slow-moving creature, with -ish.

shortDefinition: Courage and strength in facing hardship.
longDefinition: Quiet, enduring bravery — the strength to bear pain or adversity without giving way.
example: She faced the long recovery with remarkable fortitude.
secondExample: Fortitude is courage stretched over time.
useCase: Use it for steady strength through hard things.
