origin: From Latin fortitudo, “strength,” from fortis, “strong.”

longDefinition: Showing nimble, sure-handed skill, with no wasted motion.
example: A few deft strokes and the sketch was done.
secondExample: She gave a deft answer to a loaded question.
useCase: Use it for nimble, polished skill.
origin: From Old English gedæfte, “gentle, fitting” — related to daft, oddly enough.

longDefinition: Meaning what you say and feel, without falseness or flattery.
example: Please accept my sincere thanks.
secondExample: Her apology felt sincere.
useCase: Use it for honest, heartfelt genuineness.
origin: From Latin sincerus, “clean, pure, whole.”

longDefinition: Utterly firm in a decision or opinion, however much you’re pushed.
example: She was adamant that they leave before dark.
secondExample: He’s adamant about never working weekends.
useCase: Use it for unshakable, immovable resolve.
origin: From Greek adamas, “untameable,” an impossibly hard legendary stone.

longDefinition: Not boastful or showy; also moderate in amount or size.
example: She was modest about a genuinely brilliant idea.
secondExample: They live well on a modest income.
useCase: Use it for quiet, unshowy humility.
origin: From Latin modestus, “keeping due measure,” from modus, “measure.”

longDefinition: Quietly unpretentious — making no show of importance.
example: The unassuming café turned out to have the best food in town.
secondExample: He’s an unassuming expert who lets the work speak.
useCase: Use it for understated, humble presence.
origin: From un- plus assuming, “presuming” — literally not presuming importance.

shortDefinition: Pleasant and well-matched to your taste.
longDefinition: Agreeable in company, or suited to your nature and liking.
example: They found a congenial little group of regulars.
secondExample: It was congenial work in congenial company.
useCase: Use it for a comfortable, agreeable fit.
origin: From Latin com-, “together,” plus genialis, “of one’s nature.”

shortDefinition: Friendly and festive, fond of good company.
longDefinition: Cheerful and sociable, in the spirit of a shared meal or celebration.
example: The dinner had a warm, convivial buzz.
secondExample: He’s most convivial with a full table.
useCase: Use it for merry, sociable warmth.
origin: From Latin convivium, “a feast,” from com- plus vivere, “to live.”

longDefinition: Steadfastly faithful to a person, cause, or belief.
example: A staunch ally, she never wavered.
secondExample: He remained a staunch defender of the plan.
useCase: Use it for unwavering loyalty.
origin: From Old French estanche, “watertight” — solid enough to hold.

longDefinition: Resolutely fixed in place, purpose, or affection.
example: Her steadfast support got him through the year.
secondExample: They kept a steadfast course despite the storm.
useCase: Use it for steady, dependable constancy.
origin: From Old English stede, “place,” plus fæst, “fixed.”

longDefinition: Acting for others’ good without expecting anything back.
example: Her altruistic streak shows in small, quiet ways.
secondExample: It was a purely altruistic gesture.
useCase: Use it for genuine selflessness.
origin: From French altruisme, coined by Comte from Latin alter, “other.”

longDefinition: More concerned with others’ needs than your own.
example: Her selfless care for the family asked nothing in return.
secondExample: It was a selfless act no one saw.
useCase: Use it for generous self-forgetting.
origin: From self plus -less — literally “without self.”

longDefinition: Giving on a grand, openhanded scale.
example: A munificent donor funded the whole library.
secondExample: The host was munificent to a fault.
useCase: Use it for large-scale, splendid generosity.
origin: From Latin munus, “gift,” plus facere, “to make.”

longDefinition: Convinced of your own superiority and dismissive of others.
example: His arrogant tone lost the room.
secondExample: Confidence is fine; arrogance is not.
useCase: Use it for grating, superior conceit.
origin: From Latin arrogare, “to claim for oneself.”

longDefinition: Affectedly grave or grand, especially in speech.
example: He gave a pompous little lecture no one asked for.
secondExample: The writing was pompous where it should be plain.
useCase: Use it for inflated, self-regarding airs.
origin: From Latin pompa, “a grand procession.”

shortDefinition: Trying to seem more important than you are.
longDefinition: Putting on airs of greater worth, talent, or culture than is real.
example: The menu was pretentious but the food was plain.
secondExample: It sounded pretentious, so she cut the fancy words.
useCase: Use it for showy, overreaching affectation.
origin: From Latin praetendere, “to stretch in front, put forward.”

longDefinition: Looking down on others with disdainful arrogance.
example: She gave a haughty little sniff and turned away.
secondExample: His haughty manner made allies into rivals.
useCase: Use it for chilly, looking-down-the-nose pride.
origin: From Old French haut, “high,” from Latin altus.

longDefinition: Blunt to the point of seeming curt or dismissive.
example: His brusque reply ended the chat fast.
secondExample: She’s brusque on the phone but kind in person.
useCase: Use it for short, no-nonsense sharpness.
origin: From Italian brusco, “sharp, tart,” of a sour plant.

longDefinition: So short in speech or manner that it borders on impolite.
example: A curt “no” was all she got.
secondExample: His emails are curt but clear.
useCase: Use it for clipped, cool brevity.
origin: From Latin curtus, “shortened, cut off.”

longDefinition: Irritating others through a rough, scraping personality.
example: His abrasive style won arguments and lost friends.
secondExample: She softened her abrasive first drafts.
useCase: Use it for a personality that rubs people raw.
origin: From Latin abradere, “to scrape away.”

longDefinition: Grumpily difficult and quick to quarrel.
example: The cantankerous old cat tolerated no one.
secondExample: He gets cantankerous before his coffee.
useCase: Use it for crusty, quarrelsome grumpiness.
origin: Of uncertain 18th-century origin, perhaps blending “contentious.”

longDefinition: Hot-tempered, flaring up at the smallest provocation.
example: The irascible chef barked at every order.
secondExample: Exhaustion made him irascible.
useCase: Use it for a short, easily-lit fuse.
origin: From Latin irasci, “to grow angry,” from ira, “anger.”

longDefinition: Peevish and bad-tempered over small things, like a fussing child.
example: He gave a petulant shrug and stormed off.
secondExample: Her petulant mood passed by lunch.
useCase: Use it for sulky, small-minded irritability.
origin: From Latin petulans, “impudent, forward.”

longDefinition: Moody and uncommunicative, nursing displeasure quietly.
example: He sat through dinner in sullen silence.
secondExample: A sullen sky hung over the city.
useCase: Use it for brooding, withdrawn moodiness.
origin: From Latin solus, “alone” — the mood of one who withdraws.

longDefinition: Sullenly sad, withdrawn into a dark mood.
example: He turned morose after the news.
secondExample: Rainy weeks make her morose.
useCase: Use it for heavy, sulky gloom.
origin: From Latin morosus, “peevish,” from mos, “habit, temper.”

longDefinition: Severe and forbidding in look or manner.
example: The dour clerk never once smiled.
secondExample: He had a dour view of the whole affair.
useCase: Use it for grim, humorless severity.
origin: From Latin durus, “hard.”

longDefinition: Strict and self-denying, or starkly unadorned in style.
example: The monk led an austere life.
secondExample: The room was austere but beautiful.
useCase: Use it for stark, disciplined plainness.
origin: From Greek austeros, “harsh, bitter.”

longDefinition: Demanding and severe, allowing little softness.
example: A stern look was enough to quiet the class.
secondExample: She took a stern line on lateness.
useCase: Use it for firm, no-nonsense severity.
origin: From Old English styrne, “severe, strict.”

longDefinition: Full of animation and high-spirited energy.
example: Her vivacious laugh filled the room.
secondExample: He’s vivacious company on a dull day.
useCase: Use it for sparkling, animated liveliness.
origin: From Latin vivax, “lively,” from vivere, “to live.”

longDefinition: Lavishly enthusiastic, abundant, and high-spirited.
example: The crowd gave an exuberant cheer.
secondExample: Spring brought an exuberant tangle of green.
useCase: Use it for abundant, irrepressible enthusiasm.
origin: From Latin exuberare, “to be abundant,” from uber, “fertile.”

shortDefinition: Cheerful and quick to bounce back.
longDefinition: Light-hearted and resilient; also able to float.
example: She stayed buoyant despite the delays.
secondExample: The mood in the office was buoyant.
useCase: Use it for light, floating good spirits.
origin: From Spanish boyar, “to float.”

longDefinition: Peacefully untroubled in temperament or setting.
example: He has a placid, easygoing nature.
secondExample: The lake lay placid in the dawn light.
useCase: Use it for unruffled, gentle calm.
origin: From Latin placidus, “gentle, quiet,” from placere, “to please.”

longDefinition: Pleasantly gentle in mood, sound, or flavor — often softened by time.
example: He’s grown mellow with the years.
secondExample: The evening had a warm, mellow light.
useCase: Use it for soft, unhurried ease.
origin: Likely from Old English mearu, “soft,” later “ripe, soft fruit.”

longDefinition: Showing balance and restraint; also mild in climate.
example: A temperate response cooled the argument.
secondExample: They moved somewhere more temperate.
useCase: Use it for measured, moderate restraint.
origin: From Latin temperare, “to moderate, mix in proportion.”

shortDefinition: Acting on sudden urges without thinking.
longDefinition: Driven by impulse rather than careful thought.
example: An impulsive buy she didn’t regret.
secondExample: He’s impulsive, for better and worse.
useCase: Use it for spur-of-the-moment action.
origin: From Latin impellere, “to drive on.”

longDefinition: Acting without thinking through the consequences.
example: Don’t make a rash decision while you’re angry.
secondExample: It was a rash promise he couldn’t keep.
useCase: Use it for reckless haste.
origin: From Middle English rasch, “quick, hasty.”

longDefinition: Acting with no regard for risk to yourself or others.
example: Reckless spending drained the account.
secondExample: He drove with reckless speed.
useCase: Use it for heedless, risky abandon.
origin: From Old English receleas, “careless,” from reck, “to care.”

longDefinition: Considering all the circumstances and risks before you move.
example: She was circumspect about sharing the rumor.
secondExample: Be circumspect with money you can’t replace.
useCase: Use it for wary, all-around caution.
origin: From Latin circumspicere, “to look around,” circum- plus specere.

longDefinition: Watchful for danger or trickery before trusting.
example: She’s wary of deals that sound too good.
secondExample: The cat stayed wary of the new dog.
useCase: Use it for guarded, watchful caution.
origin: From Old English wær, “aware, careful.”

longDefinition: Alert and attentive, ready to catch danger early.
example: Vigilant neighbors noticed the smoke first.
secondExample: Stay vigilant with your passwords.
useCase: Use it for alert, attentive watchfulness.
origin: From Latin vigilare, “to keep awake, watch.”

shortDefinition: Precise about fine points and etiquette.
longDefinition: Showing exact, almost fussy attention to detail and correct behavior.
example: He was punctilious about thank-you notes.
secondExample: Her punctilious records left nothing to chance.
useCase: Use it for exact, proper attention to detail.
origin: From Italian puntiglio, “fine point,” from Latin punctum, “point.”

longDefinition: Showing good manners and respect for others.
example: A courteous reply, even to a rude email.
secondExample: He’s unfailingly courteous to staff.
useCase: Use it for gracious, well-mannered politeness.
origin: From Old French corteis, “having manners fit for a court.”

shortDefinition: Courteous and gallant, especially in protecting others.
longDefinition: Showing the old knightly virtues of courtesy, honor, and care.
example: A chivalrous gesture, holding the umbrella over her.
secondExample: He was chivalrous without being patronizing.
useCase: Use it for honorable, courteous gallantry.
origin: From French chevalier, “knight,” from cheval, “horse.”

longDefinition: Treating people and animals with care and dignity.
example: A humane policy that put people first.
secondExample: They argued for more humane conditions.
useCase: Use it for merciful, dignified kindness.
origin: A variant of human, from Latin humanus.

longDefinition: Diligently and steadily applying effort.
example: An industrious team cleared the backlog in a week.
secondExample: She’s industrious to the point of restlessness.
useCase: Use it for steady, productive hard work.
origin: From Latin industria, “diligence, activity.”

shortDefinition: Holding on firmly; not giving up.
longDefinition: Persistent and determined, keeping a tight grip on a goal.
example: A tenacious reporter who wouldn’t drop the story.
secondExample: Tenacious roots cracked the pavement.
useCase: Use it for stubborn, gripping persistence.
origin: From Latin tenax, “holding fast,” from tenere, “to hold.”

longDefinition: Refusing to give up, even slowly and grimly.
example: Her dogged effort finally paid off.
secondExample: Dogged practice beats raw talent.
useCase: Use it for grinding, refuse-to-quit persistence.
origin: From dog — as tenacious as a dog with a bone.

longDefinition: Diligent about doing things correctly and honestly, down to small details.
example: Scrupulous accounting kept them out of trouble.
secondExample: She’s scrupulous about crediting others.
useCase: Use it for painstaking, principled care.
origin: From Latin scrupulus, “small sharp stone” — a tiny nagging worry.

longDefinition: Straightforward in speech, holding nothing back.
example: I appreciate how forthright she is in meetings.
secondExample: Be forthright about what you need.
useCase: Use it for plain, upfront honesty.
origin: From Old English forþ, “forward,” plus riht, “straight.”

longDefinition: Acting consistently from a clear set of values.
example: A principled stand that cost him the deal.
secondExample: She’s principled even when no one’s watching.
useCase: Use it for steady moral backbone.
origin: From principle, from Latin principium, “beginning, foundation.”

shortDefinition: Open and honest, even about hard things.
longDefinition: Speaking plainly and directly, without evasion.
example: Let me be frank: it isn’t working.
secondExample: A frank talk cleared the air.
useCase: Use it for plain, unguarded honesty.
origin: From the Franks, whose freemen could speak freely.

longDefinition: Showing sharp, inventive humor in speech or writing.
example: Her witty toast had the room laughing.
secondExample: A witty reply defused the tension.
useCase: Use it for quick, clever humor.
origin: From Old English witt, “mind, intelligence.”

shortDefinition: Quick and light in movement or thought.
longDefinition: Able to move or think with speed and ease.
example: Nimble fingers untied the knot.
secondExample: A nimble mind for numbers.
useCase: Use it for quick, agile lightness.
origin: From Old English næmel, “quick to grasp.”

shortDefinition: Lively and quick, especially when older.
longDefinition: Active and nimble in a way that defies expectation.
example: Spry at eighty, she still gardens daily.
secondExample: He gave a spry little hop over the puddle.
useCase: Use it for surprising, energetic agility.
origin: Of uncertain origin, perhaps Scandinavian, “active, brisk.”

longDefinition: Trim, smart, and well-turned-out, often with a little flair.
example: He showed up dapper in a pressed linen suit.
secondExample: A dapper little bow tie completed it.
useCase: Use it for sharp, tidy style.
origin: From Middle Dutch dapper, “strong, nimble.”

longDefinition: Attractively unusual in a way that recalls another time.
example: A quaint village of crooked little lanes.
secondExample: There’s a quaint custom of leaving the door open.
useCase: Use it for old-world, endearing charm.
origin: From Old French cointe, “clever, elegant,” from Latin cognitus.

longDefinition: Lacking variety or excitement; routine to the point of boredom.
example: He wanted out of the humdrum routine.
secondExample: Even humdrum days have small pleasures.
useCase: Use it for tedious, samey routine.
origin: A reduplication of hum, suggesting a dull, droning sound.

longDefinition: Only mildly warm, or unenthusiastic and half-hearted.
example: The idea got a tepid response.
secondExample: Tepid coffee is its own small tragedy.
useCase: Use it for half-hearted, lukewarm feeling.
origin: From Latin tepidus, “lukewarm,” from tepere, “to be warm.”

shortDefinition: Sharp and clever in practical matters.
longDefinition: Quick to judge situations and act to your advantage.
example: A shrewd negotiator, she read the room instantly.
secondExample: It was a shrewd guess that paid off.
useCase: Use it for canny, practical cleverness.
origin: From Middle English shrewe, “rascal,” later “sharp, cunning.”

longDefinition: Wise and careful, especially about money or risk.
example: A canny investor who never chased hype.
secondExample: She made a canny early move.
useCase: Use it for careful, sharp-eyed cleverness.
origin: From Scots can, “to know, be able.”

longDefinition: Sharply aware of things others miss, in people or situations.
example: A perceptive question got to the real issue.
secondExample: She’s perceptive about people’s moods.
useCase: Use it for keen, noticing insight.
origin: From Latin percipere, “to seize, understand.”

longDefinition: Able to tell quality and meaning apart with a refined eye.
example: A discerning reader spots a weak argument fast.
secondExample: She has a discerning palate.
useCase: Use it for refined, judging taste.
origin: From Latin discernere, “to separate, distinguish.”

longDefinition: Revealing the true inner nature of something with clarity.
example: An insightful comment that reframed the whole debate.
secondExample: Her notes were brief but insightful.
useCase: Use it for penetrating understanding.
origin: From insight, from Middle English, “inner sight.”

longDefinition: Showing deep, calm wisdom; also a wise person.
example: Sage advice from someone who’d been there.
secondExample: The old sage spoke rarely but well.
useCase: Use it for seasoned, calm wisdom.
origin: From Latin sapere, “to be wise, to taste.”

longDefinition: Showing wide, scholarly knowledge.
example: An erudite lecture, worn lightly.
secondExample: He’s erudite without ever showing off.
useCase: Use it for deep, scholarly knowledge.
origin: From Latin eruditus, “instructed,” literally “freed from roughness.”

shortDefinition: Fussily focused on rules and details.
longDefinition: Overly concerned with minor correctness, often tediously so.
example: Spare me the pedantic footnotes.
secondExample: He got pedantic about the apostrophe.
useCase: Use it for nitpicky, rule-bound fussiness.
origin: From Italian pedante, “teacher,” later “one who overteaches.”

shortDefinition: Understood by only a small, expert few.
longDefinition: Specialized or obscure, meant for insiders.
example: The talk turned esoteric and lost the crowd.
secondExample: It’s an esoteric hobby with a devoted following.
useCase: Use it for niche, insider knowledge.
origin: From Greek esoterikos, “inner,” for teachings kept within a circle.

shortDefinition: Hard to understand; deep and obscure.
longDefinition: Difficult to grasp because it’s complex or recondite.
example: The theory is brilliant but abstruse.
secondExample: He has a gift for making abstruse ideas plain.
useCase: Use it for the dauntingly complex.
origin: From Latin abstrusus, “hidden, concealed.”

longDefinition: Secret or hidden, understood only by initiates.
example: The arcane rules of an old card game.
secondExample: He delights in arcane trivia.
useCase: Use it for secret, little-known lore.
origin: From Latin arcanus, “secret,” from arca, “chest, box.”

shortDefinition: Little known, or hard to make out.
longDefinition: Not clearly understood, or hidden from general notice.
example: An obscure poet she loved to quote.
secondExample: The meaning stays obscure on first read.
useCase: Use it for the unclear or little-known.
origin: From Latin obscurus, “dark, covered.”

shortDefinition: Believable on the face of it.
longDefinition: Seeming reasonable or likely, whether or not it’s true.
example: A plausible excuse, but not the real one.
secondExample: The theory is at least plausible.
useCase: Use it for the credibly believable.
origin: From Latin plausibilis, “deserving applause,” from plaudere.

longDefinition: Slight and insubstantial — a grip, a link, or an argument that may not hold.
example: The evidence was tenuous at best.
secondExample: They kept a tenuous connection over the years.
useCase: Use it for a flimsy, fragile link.
origin: From Latin tenuis, “thin.”

longDefinition: Uncertain about something, or hard to trust.
example: She was dubious about the shortcut.
secondExample: A dubious claim with no source.
useCase: Use it for doubt, or shady quality.
origin: From Latin dubiosus, “doubtful,” from dubius.

longDefinition: Founded on conjecture or theory rather than firm evidence.
example: It’s speculative, but the pattern is suggestive.
secondExample: A speculative bet on a new market.
useCase: Use it for educated guessing.
origin: From Latin speculari, “to observe, spy out.”

shortDefinition: Imagined for the sake of argument.
longDefinition: Supposed rather than real — a “what if” to reason about.
example: Let’s take a hypothetical case.
secondExample: That’s purely hypothetical, of course.
useCase: Use it for an imagined, test-case scenario.
origin: From Greek hypothesis, “a placing under, foundation.”

shortDefinition: About ideas rather than concrete things.
longDefinition: Existing in thought or as a concept, apart from physical examples.
example: Justice is an abstract idea until it’s tested.
secondExample: He thinks in abstract patterns.
useCase: Use it for the conceptual and intangible.
origin: From Latin abstractus, “drawn away.”

longDefinition: Based on your own views and experience rather than outside fact.
example: Taste in music is deeply subjective.
secondExample: Keep the review fair, not subjective.
useCase: Use it for the personal and felt.
origin: From Latin subiectivus, “of the one experiencing.”

longDefinition: Treating all sides equally, without favoritism.
example: An impartial judge weighed both claims.
secondExample: We need an impartial referee.
useCase: Use it for even-handed fairness.
origin: From in-, “not,” plus partial, “biased toward a part.”

longDefinition: Given to calm, deep reflection.
example: A contemplative walk to clear his head.
secondExample: She’s in a contemplative mood tonight.
useCase: Use it for calm, inward reflection.
origin: From Latin contemplari, “to gaze at, consider.”

shortDefinition: Deep in thought, often a little sad.
longDefinition: Engaged in serious, wistful reflection.
example: She grew pensive as the train pulled away.
secondExample: A pensive silence settled over them.
useCase: Use it for wistful, musing thought.
origin: From French penser, “to think,” from Latin pensare.

longDefinition: Intentional and unhurried, weighed before acting.
example: Slow, deliberate movements steadied her nerves.
secondExample: It was a deliberate choice, not an accident.
useCase: Use it for careful, intentional action.
origin: From Latin deliberare, “to weigh well,” from libra, “scales.”

shortDefinition: To understand something deep or puzzling.
longDefinition: To get to the bottom of an idea or mystery.
example: I can’t fathom why they left.
secondExample: She finally fathomed how the trick worked.
useCase: Use it for grasping the hard-to-grasp.
origin: From Old English fæðm, “outstretched arms” — once a unit for measuring depth.

shortDefinition: A guess based on incomplete evidence.
longDefinition: An opinion or conclusion formed without firm proof.
example: Without data, it’s pure conjecture.
secondExample: Her conjecture turned out to be right.
useCase: Use it for a reasoned but unproven guess.
origin: From Latin conicere, “to throw together, infer.”

shortDefinition: A state of uncertainty over a hard choice.
longDefinition: A perplexing dilemma where every option has a catch.
example: I’m in a quandary about which offer to take.
secondExample: The news left them in a real quandary.
useCase: Use it for a puzzling, no-easy-answer choice.
origin: Of uncertain 16th-century origin, perhaps mock-Latin.

longDefinition: Appealing to or driven by the intellect.
example: A cerebral film that rewards attention.
secondExample: He takes a cerebral approach to everything.
useCase: Use it for the brainy and analytical.
origin: From Latin cerebrum, “brain.”

longDefinition: Inclined to talk a great deal, easily and at length.
example: Our loquacious guide narrated every street.
secondExample: Wine made him loquacious.
useCase: Use it for free-flowing talkativeness.
origin: From Latin loqui, “to speak.”

longDefinition: Talking readily and rapidly, words rolling easily.
example: She was voluble on her favorite subject.
secondExample: A voluble defense poured out of him.
useCase: Use it for fluent, rapid talk.
origin: From Latin volubilis, “rolling, fluent,” from volvere, “to roll.”

longDefinition: Fluent and easy in speech in a way that seems insincere.
example: A glib answer that dodged the question.
secondExample: Don’t trust glib promises.
useCase: Use it for slick, hollow fluency.
origin: Probably from Low German glibberig, “slippery.”

shortDefinition: So convincing or interesting you can’t look away.
longDefinition: Powerfully persuasive or absorbing.
example: A compelling story kept the whole room still.
secondExample: She made a compelling case for change.
useCase: Use it for the gripping and persuasive.
origin: From Latin compellere, “to drive together, force.”

longDefinition: Not definite in meaning, shape, or detail.
example: His plans were vague at best.
secondExample: A vague memory, more feeling than fact.
useCase: Use it for the blurry and undefined.
origin: From Latin vagus, “wandering.”

longDefinition: Deliberately obscure or puzzling in meaning.
example: He left a cryptic note and vanished.
secondExample: Her cryptic smile gave nothing away.
useCase: Use it for puzzling, coded meaning.
origin: From Greek kryptos, “hidden.”

shortDefinition: A phrase whose meaning isn’t literal.
longDefinition: An expression peculiar to a language, like “spill the beans.”
example: “Break a leg” is an idiom for good luck.
secondExample: Idioms are the hardest part of a new language.
useCase: Use it for a figurative set phrase.
origin: From Greek idioma, “peculiar phrasing,” from idios, “own.”

longDefinition: A traditional proverb expressing a general truth.
example: The old adage “less is more” fits here.
secondExample: She lives by the adage that kindness pays.
useCase: Use it for a familiar piece of folk wisdom.
origin: From Latin adagium, “proverb.”

shortDefinition: A short, sharp statement of truth.
longDefinition: A pithy observation that captures a principle in a line.
example: “Brevity is the soul of wit” is a perfect aphorism.
secondExample: He spoke in clever aphorisms.
useCase: Use it for a crisp nugget of wisdom.
origin: From Greek aphorismos, “definition,” from aphorizein, “to mark off.”

shortDefinition: A rule of conduct stated in a line.
longDefinition: A short statement of a general truth or principle to live by.
example: “Measure twice, cut once” is his maxim.
secondExample: A good maxim survives the centuries.
useCase: Use it for a guiding rule of thumb.
origin: From Latin maxima propositio, “greatest premise.”

shortDefinition: A short traditional saying with advice.
longDefinition: A folk saying that states a truth or piece of practical wisdom.
example: There’s a proverb for every kind of foolishness.
secondExample: “Haste makes waste,” as the proverb goes.
useCase: Use it for inherited folk wisdom.
origin: From Latin proverbium, “a common saying,” pro- plus verbum, “word.”

shortDefinition: Describing one thing as if it were another.
longDefinition: A figure of speech that calls something by the name of something else to reveal a likeness.
example: “Time is a thief” is a metaphor.
secondExample: The whole poem turns on a single metaphor.
useCase: Use it for an illuminating figurative comparison.
origin: From Greek metaphora, “a carrying across.”

shortDefinition: A comparison that explains by similarity.
longDefinition: Explaining something unfamiliar by likening it to something known.
example: She used a cooking analogy to explain the code.
secondExample: The analogy breaks down if you push it.
useCase: Use it for an explaining-by-comparison.
origin: From Greek analogia, “proportion, correspondence.”

longDefinition: A passing mention that points to a person, work, or event without naming it outright.
example: The title is an allusion to an old hymn.
secondExample: His speech was full of literary allusions.
useCase: Use it for a knowing, indirect reference.
origin: From Latin alludere, “to play with, refer to.”

longDefinition: Overstatement used for effect, not meant to be taken literally.
example: “I’ve told you a million times” is hyperbole.
secondExample: Cut the hyperbole and give me the numbers.
useCase: Use it for exaggeration made for effect.
origin: From Greek hyperbolē, “a throwing beyond, excess.”

shortDefinition: Saying less than you mean, for effect.
longDefinition: Describing something as smaller or milder than it really is.
example: Calling the storm “a bit of rain” was an understatement.
secondExample: British humor runs on understatement.
useCase: Use it for deliberate, dry restraint.
origin: From under- plus statement — stating beneath the truth.

longDefinition: Extended, often formal conversation or writing about a topic.
example: The book reshaped public discourse on the issue.
secondExample: Their dinner discourse ranged over everything.
useCase: Use it for substantive discussion or debate.
origin: From Latin discursus, “a running about,” from discurrere.

longDefinition: A lively exchange of clever, fast replies.
example: Their dinner repartee was the best entertainment.
secondExample: She’s famous for her repartee.
useCase: Use it for sparkling back-and-forth wit.
origin: From French repartie, “a quick reply,” from repartir, “to retort.”

longDefinition: A pointed answer, often in response to a criticism.
example: Her retort silenced the heckler.
secondExample: He had a retort ready for everything.
useCase: Use it for a fast, cutting comeback.
origin: From Latin retorquere, “to twist back.”

shortDefinition: A reasoned reply that argues against.
longDefinition: An answer that contradicts or refutes a claim with evidence.
example: Her rebuttal dismantled the argument point by point.
secondExample: The defense offered a calm rebuttal.
useCase: Use it for a counterargument that answers back.
origin: From Old French rebuter, “to thrust back.”

longDefinition: Great happiness and high spirits, especially after success.
example: There was pure elation when the offer came through.
secondExample: Her elation lasted all week.
useCase: Use it for lifted, triumphant joy.
origin: From Latin elatus, “lifted up,” from efferre, “to carry out.”

longDefinition: Showing great, open happiness at a victory or success.
example: Jubilant fans poured into the streets.
secondExample: She sent a jubilant text the moment she heard.
useCase: Use it for celebratory, exultant joy.
origin: From Latin jubilare, “to shout for joy.”

longDefinition: Filled with rapturous delight, almost beside yourself.
example: She was ecstatic about the news.
secondExample: An ecstatic crowd sang along.
useCase: Use it for overwhelming, blissful joy.
origin: From Greek ekstasis, “standing outside oneself.”

longDefinition: Light-headed and excitable, often happily so.
example: They were giddy with relief.
secondExample: A giddy rush of first love.
useCase: Use it for light-headed, excited happiness.
origin: From Old English gydig, “insane, possessed by a god.”

shortDefinition: Full of delighted, often mischievous joy.
longDefinition: Openly merry, sometimes with a spark of playful triumph.
example: A gleeful grin spread across her face.
secondExample: He was gleeful about the surprise.
useCase: Use it for bright, playful delight.
origin: From Old English gleo, “entertainment, music, joy.”

longDefinition: Pitifully abandoned or hopeless in feeling or appearance.
example: A forlorn dog waited by the gate.
secondExample: She felt forlorn in the empty house.
useCase: Use it for lonely, abandoned sadness.
origin: From Old English forloren, “utterly lost.”

longDefinition: Barren and abandoned, or feeling utterly forsaken.
example: A desolate stretch of windswept coast.
secondExample: She felt desolate after they left.
useCase: Use it for bleak emptiness, inside or out.
origin: From Latin desolare, “to abandon,” from solus, “alone.”

shortDefinition: In low spirits from loss of hope.
longDefinition: Disheartened and downcast, feeling there’s no way forward.
example: He grew despondent after the third rejection.
secondExample: Don’t let one setback leave you despondent.
useCase: Use it for hope-drained low spirits.
origin: From Latin despondere, “to give up, lose heart.”

longDefinition: Cast down and disheartened, often visibly so.
example: He looked dejected after the loss.
secondExample: A dejected slump of the shoulders.
useCase: Use it for downcast disappointment.
origin: From Latin deicere, “to throw down.”

longDefinition: Dejected after a hope is dashed, head and spirits drooping.
example: She left the audition crestfallen.
secondExample: He was crestfallen at the news.
useCase: Use it for a deflated, just-let-down mood.
origin: From a bird or animal whose raised crest has fallen.

shortDefinition: Dark, grave, and serious in mood.
longDefinition: Gloomily solemn, in color, tone, or feeling.
example: A somber mood settled over the room.
secondExample: They wore somber colors to the service.
useCase: Use it for grave, shadowed seriousness.
origin: From French sombre, “dark,” perhaps from Latin sub umbra, “under shade.”

shortDefinition: Happy and sad at the same time.
longDefinition: Pleasant and painful at once, like a fond goodbye.
example: Graduation is always bittersweet.
secondExample: A bittersweet ending that felt just right.
useCase: Use it for joy mixed with sorrow.
origin: From bitter plus sweet — the two tastes held together.

longDefinition: Readily expressing tenderness and love.
example: An affectionate note tucked in the bag.
secondExample: They’re openly affectionate with each other.
useCase: Use it for warm, loving tenderness.
origin: From Latin affectio, “a disposition, fondness.”

longDefinition: Feeling warm liking or love toward someone or something.
example: She’s fond of long Sunday walks.
secondExample: A fond farewell at the station.
useCase: Use it for gentle, warm attachment.
origin: From Middle English fonned, “foolish, doting.”

longDefinition: Struck with affection or infatuation, often at once.
example: He was smitten from the first hello.
secondExample: She’s smitten with the new puppy.
useCase: Use it for being instantly charmed.
origin: Past participle of smite, “to strike” — struck by love.

longDefinition: Charmed and captivated by someone or something.
example: She’s thoroughly enamored of the old city.
secondExample: He was enamored with the idea from the start.
useCase: Use it for being captivated or in love.
origin: From Old French enamourer, from Latin amor, “love.”

longDefinition: A consuming but often shallow attraction that burns hot and fades.
example: It was infatuation, not love — and it passed.
secondExample: A teenage infatuation she now laughs about.
useCase: Use it for a fervent, fleeting crush.
origin: From Latin infatuare, “to make a fool of.”

longDefinition: Burning with intense feeling, devotion, or zeal.
example: An ardent supporter from day one.
secondExample: Her ardent love of the sea shaped her life.
useCase: Use it for fiery, devoted passion.
origin: From Latin ardere, “to burn.”

longDefinition: Showing great warmth of feeling or belief.
example: A fervent hope that things would change.
secondExample: Her fervent thanks moved everyone.
useCase: Use it for heartfelt, burning intensity.
origin: From Latin fervere, “to boil, glow.”

longDefinition: A deep yearning for something or someone out of reach.
example: A quiet longing for the place she grew up.
secondExample: There was longing in the way he spoke of it.
useCase: Use it for steady, aching desire.
origin: From Old English langian, “to grow long, to yearn.”

longDefinition: Coming straight from genuine emotion.
example: A heartfelt thank-you that meant it.
secondExample: Her heartfelt apology repaired things.
useCase: Use it for sincere, deeply-meant feeling.
origin: From heart plus felt — felt in the heart.

shortDefinition: Guided by tender feeling and nostalgia.
longDefinition: Prone to emotion and fond memory, sometimes excessively.
example: She’s sentimental about old letters.
secondExample: A sentimental song that always gets him.
useCase: Use it for tender, nostalgic feeling.
origin: From Latin sentire, “to feel.”

shortDefinition: Unable to stay still or settled.
longDefinition: Uneasy and fidgety, craving change or movement.
example: He felt restless after a week indoors.
secondExample: A restless night of half-sleep.
useCase: Use it for unsettled, can’t-sit-still energy.
origin: From Old English ræst, “rest,” plus -less.

longDefinition: Disturbed and unable to be calm, often pacing or fretting.
example: She seemed agitated, checking the door again.
secondExample: The crowd grew agitated as the wait dragged.
useCase: Use it for stirred-up, anxious unrest.
origin: From Latin agitare, “to set in motion, drive.”

longDefinition: Agitated to the point of muddled thinking or speech.
example: He got flustered and forgot his lines.
secondExample: The sudden question left her flustered.
useCase: Use it for rattled, off-balance nerves.
origin: Perhaps Scandinavian, related to “flush, hurry.”

longDefinition: Free from disturbance; peacefully still.
example: A tranquil garden behind the house.
secondExample: She felt tranquil for the first time in weeks.
useCase: Use it for deep, settled peace.
origin: From Latin tranquillus, “calm, still.”

longDefinition: Feeling righteous anger at mistreatment or injustice.
example: She was indignant at being overlooked.
secondExample: An indignant letter to the editor.
useCase: Use it for justified, principled anger.
origin: From Latin indignari, “to regard as unworthy.”

shortDefinition: Deep regret for a wrong you’ve done.
longDefinition: A gnawing guilt and sorrow over past actions.
example: He felt real remorse and tried to make amends.
secondExample: Not a flicker of remorse crossed her face.
useCase: Use it for guilt-edged regret.
origin: From Latin remordere, “to bite back.”

longDefinition: Strongly wanting success, or large in scope and aim.
example: An ambitious plan to double in a year.
secondExample: She’s ambitious but never ruthless.
useCase: Use it for big, driving aspiration.
origin: From Latin ambitio, “a striving for favor,” literally “a going around.”

longDefinition: Propelled by a strong inner need to achieve.
example: A driven founder who slept at the office.
secondExample: She’s driven, but knows when to rest.
useCase: Use it for intense inner motivation.
origin: Past participle of drive, from Old English drifan.

longDefinition: Working productively with minimal wasted time or effort.
example: An efficient routine that freed up her mornings.
secondExample: The new tool made the team more efficient.
useCase: Use it for lean, productive working.
origin: From Latin efficere, “to accomplish.”

shortDefinition: Capable and good enough at the job.
longDefinition: Having the skill to do something properly and reliably.
example: A competent, steady hand on the project.
secondExample: She felt competent after the training.
useCase: Use it for reliable, sufficient skill.
origin: From Latin competere, “to be suitable.”

longDefinition: Competent to a high standard in a particular skill.
example: She’s proficient in three languages.
secondExample: Proficient enough to teach it now.
useCase: Use it for well-practiced skill.
origin: From Latin proficere, “to make progress.”

longDefinition: Quick and deft in handling situations or tasks.
example: An adroit answer that satisfied everyone.
secondExample: She’s adroit at defusing conflict.
useCase: Use it for nimble, clever skill.
origin: From French à droit, “rightly, properly.”

shortDefinition: Skillful with the hands or mind.
longDefinition: Showing neat, nimble skill and control.
example: Dexterous fingers shaped the clay.
secondExample: A dexterous bit of scheduling.
useCase: Use it for nimble, handy skill.
origin: From Latin dexter, “right-hand, skillful.”

longDefinition: Sharp, real-world understanding of how things work.
example: Her business savvy saved the launch.
secondExample: He has real savvy about people.
useCase: Use it for street-smart practical sense.
origin: From a creole sabi, from Portuguese saber, “to know.”

longDefinition: Creatively new — improving on what came before.
example: An innovative fix nobody had tried.
secondExample: They’re known for innovative design.
useCase: Use it for inventive, original advances.
origin: From Latin innovare, “to renew, change.”

shortDefinition: First to explore or develop something.
longDefinition: Leading the way into new territory or methods.
example: Her pioneering research opened the field.
secondExample: A pioneering little company, years ahead.
useCase: Use it for trailblazing firsts.
origin: From pioneer, from Old French peonier, “foot soldier” who goes first.

longDefinition: Marked by bold, original ideas about the future.
example: A visionary plan for the whole district.
secondExample: She’s a visionary, if an impatient one.
useCase: Use it for bold, future-minded imagination.
origin: From Latin visio, “sight,” from videre, “to see.”

shortDefinition: Planned with the big picture in mind.
longDefinition: Designed to achieve a long-term goal through deliberate planning.
example: A strategic hire that paid off for years.
secondExample: Be strategic about where you spend energy.
useCase: Use it for far-sighted, deliberate planning.
origin: From Greek stratēgia, “generalship.”

longDefinition: Settling things clearly, or being the factor that settles them.
example: A decisive leader who didn’t dither.
secondExample: The third goal was decisive.
useCase: Use it for firm, settling action.
origin: From Latin decidere, “to cut off, decide.”

longDefinition: Expressing your needs and views firmly, without aggression.
example: She was assertive about her boundaries.
secondExample: Be assertive, not pushy.
useCase: Use it for confident self-advocacy.
origin: From Latin asserere, “to claim, affirm.”

longDefinition: Done fully and attentively, covering every part.
example: A thorough review caught the typo.
secondExample: She’s thorough to the last detail.
useCase: Use it for exhaustive, careful completeness.
origin: From Old English þuruh, “through” — done all the way through.

longDefinition: Demanding careful precision and high standards.
example: A rigorous test of the whole system.
secondExample: Rigorous training paid off in the race.
useCase: Use it for demanding, precise standards.
origin: From Latin rigor, “stiffness, severity.”

longDefinition: Requiring high standards and precise, painstaking work.
example: An exacting editor who misses nothing.
secondExample: It’s exacting work, but worth it.
useCase: Use it for high-bar, demanding precision.
origin: From Latin exigere, “to demand, drive out.”

longDefinition: Doing things at the agreed or proper time.
example: Always punctual, she arrived to the minute.
secondExample: Punctual replies keep the team moving.
useCase: Use it for dependable timeliness.
origin: From Latin punctum, “point” — keeping to the precise point in time.

longDefinition: Responsible and required to explain or justify what you do.
example: Leaders should be accountable for results.
secondExample: He held himself accountable for the slip.
useCase: Use it for owned responsibility.
origin: From account, from Old French aconter, “to reckon.”

shortDefinition: A trusted guide who teaches by example.
longDefinition: An experienced person who advises and develops someone less experienced.
example: Her mentor talked her out of quitting.
secondExample: He became a mentor to younger staff.
useCase: Use it for a wise, guiding teacher.
origin: From Mentor, the trusted advisor in Homer’s Odyssey.

shortDefinition: To be exceptionally good at something.
longDefinition: To surpass others or your own past, standing out in skill.
example: She excels at untangling messy problems.
secondExample: He excelled the moment he was given room.
useCase: Use it for standing out, outperforming.
origin: From Latin excellere, “to rise above, surpass.”

longDefinition: To flourish, especially financially or in success.
example: The shop prospered once word spread.
secondExample: Give the idea room and it will prosper.
useCase: Use it for thriving and flourishing.
origin: From Latin prosperare, “to make fortunate.”

longDefinition: Moving or changing nimbly, in body or in plans.
example: An agile team that pivots fast.
secondExample: Agile and light on her feet.
useCase: Use it for nimble adaptability.
origin: From Latin agilis, “quick, nimble,” from agere, “to do.”

longDefinition: Able to withstand stress or hold up under demand.
example: A robust system that rarely fails.
secondExample: Robust health let him bounce back fast.
useCase: Use it for tough, reliable strength.
origin: From Latin robustus, “strong as oak,” from robur, “oak.”

longDefinition: Continued effort despite obstacles, delay, or discouragement.
example: Sheer perseverance got the book finished.
secondExample: Perseverance beats brilliance over time.
useCase: Use it for keeping on through hardship.
origin: From Latin perseverare, “to persist.”

longDefinition: Strength of character to keep going through hard things.
example: It took grit to finish the last mile.
secondExample: She has more grit than anyone I know.
useCase: Use it for gutsy, enduring resolve.
origin: From Old English greot, “sand, gravel” — figuratively, toughness.

longDefinition: Notable expertise or daring, especially shown in action.
example: Her technical prowess impressed the panel.
secondExample: Athletic prowess runs in the family.
useCase: Use it for standout skill or ability.
origin: From Old French proesse, “bravery,” from prou, “valiant.”

shortDefinition: Extremely dry; lacking moisture or life.
longDefinition: Parched and barren; also dull and lacking interest.
example: The arid plain stretched to the horizon.
secondExample: An arid report with no spark.
useCase: Use it for the parched and lifeless.
origin: From Latin aridus, “dry,” from arere, “to be dry.”

longDefinition: Growing thickly and luxuriantly; full and rich.
example: Lush ferns crowded the riverbank.
secondExample: A lush, layered soundtrack.
useCase: Use it for rich, abundant growth.
origin: Perhaps from Middle English lush, “slack, soft,” later “luxuriant.”

longDefinition: Of weather, soft and soothing in temperature.
example: A balmy evening perfect for the porch.
secondExample: Balmy breezes drifted off the sea.
useCase: Use it for gentle, mild warmth.
origin: From balm, soothing aromatic resin, from Latin balsamum.

longDefinition: Intensely cold; also cold in manner.
example: A frigid wind cut through the square.
secondExample: She gave a frigid little nod.
useCase: Use it for biting cold, literal or social.
origin: From Latin frigidus, “cold,” from frigus, “frost.”

longDefinition: Sending out light or warmth — and used of faces lit by happiness.
example: A radiant sunrise over the bay.
secondExample: She was radiant on the day.
useCase: Use it for glowing, beaming brightness.
origin: From Latin radiare, “to emit beams,” from radius, “ray.”

longDefinition: Of the cosmos; also vast or grand beyond ordinary scale.
example: Cosmic distances are hard to imagine.
secondExample: A cosmic joke, the timing of it.
useCase: Use it for the universe-scale and vast.
origin: From Greek kosmos, “order, world, universe.”

longDefinition: Belonging to the planet Earth, or living on land.
example: Terrestrial animals, not marine ones.
secondExample: Earthly, terrestrial concerns.
useCase: Use it for earthbound, land-based things.
origin: From Latin terrestris, from terra, “earth.”

longDefinition: Relating to water, or to creatures and plants that dwell there.
example: Aquatic plants ringed the pond.
secondExample: An aquatic sport she took up late.
useCase: Use it for the watery and water-dwelling.
origin: From Latin aqua, “water.”

shortDefinition: Moving seasonally from place to place.
longDefinition: Given to migration, like birds traveling with the seasons.
example: Migratory birds passed overhead in autumn.
secondExample: A migratory way of life follows the work.
useCase: Use it for seasonal, traveling movement.
origin: From Latin migrare, “to move, wander.”

longDefinition: Relating to ice and glaciers; also cold in manner or painfully slow.
example: Progress moved at a glacial pace.
secondExample: A glacial silence filled the room.
useCase: Use it for icy cold or crawling slowness.
origin: From Latin glacies, “ice.”

longDefinition: Relating to ground tremors, or to a change of enormous impact.
example: A seismic shift in the industry.
secondExample: Seismic sensors picked up the tremor.
useCase: Use it for earth-shaking impact.
origin: From Greek seismos, “earthquake,” from seiein, “to shake.”

shortDefinition: Of the rise and fall of the sea.
longDefinition: Relating to tides, or rising and falling in great waves.
example: Tidal pools left behind by the sea.
secondExample: A tidal wave of applications.
useCase: Use it for tide-driven ebb and flow.
origin: From tide, from Old English tid, “time, season.”

longDefinition: Of, caused by, or measured by the moon.
example: A lunar eclipse turned the moon copper.
secondExample: The lunar calendar guides the festival.
useCase: Use it for moon-related things.
origin: From Latin luna, “moon.”

shortDefinition: Glowing colored lights in the polar sky.
longDefinition: The shimmering bands of light near the poles — the northern or southern lights.
example: The aurora rippled green across the night.
secondExample: They drove north to chase the aurora.
useCase: Use it for the polar light show.
origin: From Latin aurora, “dawn,” the Roman goddess of sunrise.

longDefinition: The peak or culmination — of the sky, a career, or a moment.
example: The empire reached its zenith that century.
secondExample: Her career hit its zenith young.
useCase: Use it for the very top or peak.
origin: From Arabic samt, “path (over the head),” via Old Spanish.

longDefinition: The bottom — the moment of deepest decline or despair.
example: That winter was the nadir of his fortunes.
secondExample: From the nadir, the only way was up.
useCase: Use it for the absolute low point.
origin: From Arabic nazir, “opposite (to the zenith).”

shortDefinition: The year’s longest or shortest day.
longDefinition: Either of the two points when the sun reaches its highest or lowest path.
example: They gathered for the summer solstice.
secondExample: After the winter solstice, the light returns.
useCase: Use it for the turning of the year’s light.
origin: From Latin sol, “sun,” plus sistere, “to stand still.”

longDefinition: To develop healthily and successfully; also a showy gesture.
example: The garden flourished after the rains.
secondExample: She signed it with a flourish.
useCase: Use it for thriving, blooming growth.
origin: From Latin florere, “to bloom,” from flos, “flower.”

shortDefinition: A system run by officials and rigid procedure.
longDefinition: Administration by departments and rules, often slow and impersonal.
example: The permit was lost in bureaucracy for months.
secondExample: Cutting bureaucracy freed the team to act.
useCase: Use it for red-tape officialdom.
origin: From French bureau, “desk, office,” plus Greek -kratia, “rule.”

longDefinition: A ruling class of inherited rank and privilege.
example: The old aristocracy lost its land but not its airs.
secondExample: An aristocracy of talent, not birth.
useCase: Use it for the privileged upper class.
origin: From Greek aristos, “best,” plus kratia, “rule.”

shortDefinition: The most powerful or skilled few.
longDefinition: A select group regarded as the best in a field or society.
example: A small elite controlled the trade.
secondExample: She trained with the sport’s elite.
useCase: Use it for the top, select tier.
origin: From French élite, “chosen,” from Latin eligere, “to pick out.”

shortDefinition: A set sequence of meaningful actions.
longDefinition: A customary or ceremonial routine performed the same way each time.
example: Her morning ritual is coffee and the crossword.
secondExample: The ceremony followed an ancient ritual.
useCase: Use it for a meaningful, repeated routine.
origin: From Latin ritualis, from ritus, “religious observance.”

shortDefinition: What’s handed down from the past.
longDefinition: The traditions, culture, and inheritance passed through generations.
example: They’re proud of their coastal heritage.
secondExample: The building is protected as heritage.
useCase: Use it for inherited culture and legacy.
origin: From Old French heriter, “to inherit,” from Latin heres, “heir.”

shortDefinition: A line of ancestors or descent.
longDefinition: The sequence of people, ideas, or things descended from a source.
example: She traced her lineage back four centuries.
secondExample: A clear lineage links the two theories.
useCase: Use it for an ancestral or descended line.
origin: From Latin linea, “line.”

shortDefinition: A line of rulers or leaders from one family.
longDefinition: A succession of powerful people, often related, who dominate over time.
example: The team built a dynasty over a decade.
secondExample: A publishing dynasty spanning three generations.
useCase: Use it for a lasting family or run of dominance.
origin: From Greek dynastes, “ruler, lord.”

shortDefinition: A system of government or management.
longDefinition: A ruling order, or an ordered way of running something.
example: The new regime changed the rules overnight.
secondExample: A strict training regime.
useCase: Use it for a governing system or strict routine.
origin: From Latin regimen, “rule, guidance,” from regere, “to rule.”

shortDefinition: A belief held as unquestionably true.
longDefinition: A principle laid down by an authority as beyond dispute.
example: He questioned the dogma everyone took for granted.
secondExample: Science should resist turning into dogma.
useCase: Use it for fixed, unchallenged belief.
origin: From Greek dogma, “opinion, decree,” from dokein, “to seem good.”

shortDefinition: A system of beliefs that guides a group.
longDefinition: A connected set of ideas shaping politics, economics, or a worldview.
example: Two parties, two opposing ideologies.
secondExample: He distrusts any rigid ideology.
useCase: Use it for a guiding belief-system.
origin: From Greek idea plus -logia, “study of ideas.”

shortDefinition: A set of taught beliefs or principles.
longDefinition: A body of teachings held by a group, church, or government.
example: The court cited a long-standing legal doctrine.
secondExample: Military doctrine shaped the plan.
useCase: Use it for an established body of teaching.
origin: From Latin doctrina, “teaching,” from docere, “to teach.”

longDefinition: Regarded as set apart and worthy of reverence.
example: A sacred grove the village protected.
secondExample: Their Sunday breakfast is sacred.
useCase: Use it for the holy or deeply cherished.
origin: From Latin sacrare, “to consecrate,” from sacer, “holy.”

longDefinition: Sincerely committed to faith or to a cause.
example: A devout follower of the old rituals.
secondExample: He’s a devout fan of the early records.
useCase: Use it for earnest, faithful devotion.
origin: From Latin devovere, “to vow, dedicate.”

shortDefinition: A culture’s body of traditional stories.
longDefinition: The collected myths of a people, or a set of widely-held beliefs.
example: Greek mythology still shapes our language.
secondExample: A whole mythology grew around the band.
useCase: Use it for a body of myth or legend.
origin: From Greek mythos, “story,” plus -logia, “study.”

longDefinition: The ideas, tastes, or activities accepted by most people.
example: The style crossed into the mainstream.
secondExample: She prefers what’s outside the mainstream.
useCase: Use it for the conventional majority.
origin: From main plus stream — the principal current of a river.

longDefinition: Behavior that matches the standards or expectations of a group.
example: There’s pressure toward conformity in big firms.
secondExample: She resisted conformity from a young age.
useCase: Use it for fitting in with the norm.
origin: From Latin conformare, “to shape after, adapt.”

shortDefinition: An independent thinker who breaks from the herd.
longDefinition: Someone who refuses to follow the usual rules or group.
example: A maverick who ignored the playbook.
secondExample: The lab’s resident maverick was usually right.
useCase: Use it for a bold nonconformist.
origin: From Samuel Maverick, a rancher who didn’t brand his cattle.

shortDefinition: A person who roams rather than settles.
longDefinition: Someone with no fixed home, moving from place to place.
example: A digital nomad working from cafés worldwide.
secondExample: Ancient nomads followed the herds.
useCase: Use it for the roaming and unsettled.
origin: From Greek nomas, “wandering for pasture.”

longDefinition: Having an abundant supply of money or possessions.
example: An affluent neighborhood of wide lawns.
secondExample: They grew up comfortable, even affluent.
useCase: Use it for prosperous wealth.
origin: From Latin affluere, “to flow toward, abound.”

longDefinition: Delightfully peaceful, like paradise.
example: An elysian afternoon in the orchard.
secondExample: The valley had an elysian calm.
useCase: Use it for paradise-like bliss.
origin: From the Elysian Fields, the Greek paradise of the blessed.

longDefinition: Evoking the gentle charm of the countryside.
example: A bucolic scene of sheep and stone walls.
secondExample: They retired to a bucolic little farm.
useCase: Use it for idyllic countryside charm.
origin: From Greek boukolos, “cowherd.”

longDefinition: Like an idyll — charmingly simple and serene.
example: An idyllic week by the lake.
secondExample: It looked idyllic in the photos.
useCase: Use it for picture-perfect peace.
origin: From Greek eidyllion, “a little picture, short pastoral poem.”

longDefinition: Surpassing the usual — sublime, or beyond ordinary experience.
example: A transcendent performance that hushed the hall.
secondExample: A moment of transcendent calm.
useCase: Use it for the surpassingly sublime.
origin: From Latin transcendere, “to climb over, surpass.”

longDefinition: Showing a play of shifting, pearly hues like an opal.
example: The sky turned opalescent at dusk.
secondExample: Opalescent glass caught the light.
useCase: Use it for soft, pearly shimmer.
origin: From opal, the gem, via Sanskrit upala, “precious stone.”

shortDefinition: Bringing strong images or memories to mind.
longDefinition: Powerfully calling up feelings, memories, or impressions.
example: An evocative smell of woodsmoke and rain.
secondExample: Her evocative prose put you right there.
useCase: Use it for vividly memory-stirring things.
origin: From Latin evocare, “to call forth.”

longDefinition: Poignant and evocative in a way that lingers in the mind.
example: A haunting melody that stayed with her for days.
secondExample: The film’s haunting final image.
useCase: Use it for lingering, poignant beauty.
origin: From haunt, from Old French hanter, “to frequent.”

longDefinition: Lasting only a brief moment before it’s gone.
example: A fleeting glimpse of the comet.
secondExample: Happiness can feel fleeting.
useCase: Use it for the brief and quickly-gone.
origin: From Old English fleotan, “to float, flow, fly.”

shortDefinition: Quick to change mood; lively and volatile.
longDefinition: Unpredictable and changeable, like quicksilver.
example: A mercurial temperament, sunny then stormy.
secondExample: The market’s mercurial swings.
useCase: Use it for fast-shifting, volatile moods.
origin: From Mercury, the swift Roman messenger god and the liquid metal.

longDefinition: Pleasingly gentle and melodious in sound.
example: The dulcet tones of the cello.
secondExample: She has a dulcet, calming voice.
useCase: Use it for sweet, soothing sound.
origin: From Latin dulcis, “sweet.”

longDefinition: Relating to or full of melody; agreeable to the ear.
example: A melodic line you can hum at once.
secondExample: Her melodic accent was a pleasure to hear.
useCase: Use it for tuneful, musical sound.
origin: From Greek melōdia, “singing, song.”

longDefinition: Free from conflict; combining into a pleasing whole.
example: A harmonious blend of old and new.
secondExample: They kept a harmonious household.
useCase: Use it for pleasing balance and accord.
origin: From Greek harmonia, “joining, agreement.”

longDefinition: Charmingly odd or imaginative, guided by whim.
example: A whimsical garden full of tiny doors.
secondExample: She has a whimsical sense of humor.
useCase: Use it for playful, fanciful charm.
origin: From whim, plus -sical, suggesting playful caprice.

longDefinition: Inspiring a sense of the sacred or supernatural beyond reason.
example: A mystical hush fell over the standing stones.
secondExample: She had a mystical view of the sea.
useCase: Use it for the spiritually mysterious.
origin: From Greek mystikos, “of the mysteries,” from mystes, “initiate.”

shortDefinition: Delightfully charming, as if by magic.
longDefinition: Captivating and lovely enough to seem spellbinding.
example: An enchanting little town in the hills.
secondExample: She found his stories enchanting.
useCase: Use it for spellbinding charm.
origin: From Latin incantare, “to sing a spell over.”

longDefinition: Drawing you in with charm or promise.
example: An alluring offer that was hard to refuse.
secondExample: The city had an alluring nighttime glow.
useCase: Use it for magnetic, tempting appeal.
origin: From Old French aleurer, “to attract,” from lure, the falconer’s bait.

longDefinition: Given or spent in great, even extravagant, quantity.
example: A lavish feast that lasted hours.
secondExample: Lavish praise that embarrassed her.
useCase: Use it for opulent abundance.
origin: From Old French lavasse, “downpour,” from laver, “to wash.”

longDefinition: Richly adorned with intricate detail.
example: An ornate ceiling of gilded vines.
secondExample: His prose is ornate where hers is spare.
useCase: Use it for elaborate, decorated detail.
origin: From Latin ornare, “to adorn.”

shortDefinition: A sudden sharp shock or jerk.
longDefinition: An abrupt movement or surprise that shakes you.
example: The news gave him a real jolt.
secondExample: The train stopped with a jolt.
useCase: Use it for a sudden jarring shock.
origin: Perhaps a blend of jot and jerk, of imitative origin.

longDefinition: A brief or incomplete sight of something.
example: She caught a glimpse of the sea between buildings.
secondExample: A glimpse of his old self returned.
useCase: Use it for a fleeting, partial view.
origin: From Middle English glimsen, “to shine faintly.”

longDefinition: A calm stillness, especially one that falls over a room or crowd.
example: A hush fell as the lights dimmed.
secondExample: There was a hush over the snowy street.
useCase: Use it for a soft, settling silence.
origin: From Middle English huisht, an imitative call for quiet.

longDefinition: Robust and dependable, not easily broken or shaken.
example: A sturdy table that survived three moves.
secondExample: Sturdy boots for rough ground.
useCase: Use it for solid, dependable strength.
origin: From Old French estourdi, “reckless,” later “robust.”

longDefinition: Insubstantial in build or, of an excuse, unconvincing.
example: A flimsy umbrella that died in the first gust.
secondExample: He gave a flimsy excuse.
useCase: Use it for the weak and insubstantial.
origin: Perhaps from film, “thin skin,” reshaped in the 18th century.

shortDefinition: To spend freely on a treat.
longDefinition: To indulge in a burst of extravagant spending.
example: They splurged on a fancy dinner.
secondExample: Once a year she splurges on good shoes.
useCase: Use it for a happy, indulgent spend.
origin: American English, of imitative origin, “a showy display.”

longDefinition: Using resources wisely and avoiding waste.
example: Thrifty shopping kept them out of debt.
secondExample: A thrifty cook wastes nothing.
useCase: Use it for resourceful, careful saving.
origin: From thrift, “prosperity,” from Old Norse thrifask, “to thrive.”

longDefinition: To waver indecisively, fussing instead of acting.
example: Stop dithering and pick one.
secondExample: He dithered until the chance was gone.
useCase: Use it for nervous, stalling indecision.
origin: A variant of didder, “to tremble.”

longDefinition: To move or fuss fidgetily out of nervousness or boredom.
example: He fidgeted with his pen all meeting.
secondExample: She fidgets when she’s anxious.
useCase: Use it for restless little movements.
origin: From obsolete fidge, “to twitch, move restlessly.”

longDefinition: To take deliberate pleasure in a taste, moment, or feeling.
example: Savor the last warm days of the year.
secondExample: He savored every bite.
useCase: Use it for relishing something slowly.
origin: From Latin sapor, “taste, flavor.”

longDefinition: Possible and practical to carry out.
example: Is the deadline even feasible?
secondExample: A feasible plan, if we start now.
useCase: Use it for what’s realistically doable.
origin: From Old French faisable, “doable,” from Latin facere, “to do.”

shortDefinition: To make as effective as possible.
longDefinition: To improve something to its best, most efficient state.
example: They optimized the route to save an hour.
secondExample: Don’t optimize what doesn’t matter.
useCase: Use it for tuning something to its best.
origin: From Latin optimus, “best.”

shortDefinition: A driving force that gets things moving.
longDefinition: The momentum or motivation behind an action or change.
example: The grant gave the project fresh impetus.
secondExample: Her arrival was the impetus we needed.
useCase: Use it for the push that starts momentum.
origin: From Latin impetere, “to attack, rush at.”

longDefinition: A steady decision to do something, held against pressure.
example: Her resolve never wavered.
secondExample: It took resolve to start over.
useCase: Use it for steady, set determination.
origin: From Latin resolvere, “to loosen, settle.”

longDefinition: The drive toward success, or the goal you reach for.
example: Her ambition was to run her own lab.
secondExample: Quiet ambition, steadily pursued.
useCase: Use it for the hunger to achieve.
origin: From Latin ambitio, “a striving for favor.”

shortDefinition: Deep skill or knowledge in a field.
longDefinition: The mastery that comes from long experience and study.
example: We brought in someone with real expertise.
secondExample: Her expertise settled the debate.
useCase: Use it for hard-won, specialist skill.
origin: From French expert, from Latin expertus, “tried, proven.”

longDefinition: Thorough, expert control over a subject or craft.
example: Years of practice led to real mastery.
secondExample: He plays with effortless mastery.
useCase: Use it for total, expert command.
origin: From Old French maistrie, from Latin magister, “master.”

shortDefinition: A natural ability to learn or do.
longDefinition: An inborn talent that makes a skill come easily.
example: She has a real aptitude for languages.
secondExample: His aptitude for numbers showed early.
useCase: Use it for natural, trainable talent.
origin: From Latin aptitudo, “fitness,” from aptus, “fitted.”

longDefinition: A serious effort or undertaking toward a goal.
example: A bold endeavor that took years.
secondExample: We wish you well in this endeavor.
useCase: Use it for a determined undertaking.
origin: From Middle English endeveren, “to exert oneself.”

longDefinition: A project with uncertain outcome, taken on for possible reward.
example: Their new venture surprised everyone.
secondExample: It was a venture into unknown territory.
useCase: Use it for a bold, risky undertaking.
origin: Shortened from adventure, from Latin advenire, “to arrive.”

shortDefinition: An idea you build an argument on.
longDefinition: A statement assumed true as the basis for reasoning.
example: The whole plan rests on a shaky premise.
secondExample: Start from a clear premise.
useCase: Use it for a foundational assumption.
origin: From Latin praemissa, “(things) sent before.”

longDefinition: The underlying logic or set of reasons for something.
example: Walk me through the rationale.
secondExample: The rationale was sound, the timing wasn’t.
useCase: Use it for the why behind a choice.
origin: From Latin rationalis, “of reason.”

shortDefinition: A hard choice between two options.
longDefinition: A situation forcing a choice where neither path is fully good.
example: She faced the classic dilemma of work versus home.
secondExample: An ethical dilemma with no clean answer.
useCase: Use it for a tough either-or choice.
origin: From Greek di-, “two,” plus lēmma, “premise.”

shortDefinition: Vague and hard to pin down.
longDefinition: Unclear in form or meaning, like a drifting cloud.
example: His plans were still nebulous.
secondExample: A nebulous sense that something was off.
useCase: Use it for the hazy and undefined.
origin: From Latin nebula, “mist, cloud.”

longDefinition: A loosely formed concept or sudden whim.
example: He had a notion to take the long way home.
secondExample: Where did you get that notion?
useCase: Use it for a loose idea or fancy.
origin: From Latin notio, “concept,” from noscere, “to know.”

shortDefinition: To make something out or tell apart.
longDefinition: To perceive or distinguish, especially with effort.
example: I could just discern a shape in the fog.
secondExample: It’s hard to discern his real motive.
useCase: Use it for perceiving or distinguishing.
origin: From Latin discernere, “to separate, distinguish.”

longDefinition: To suppose something is true without proof.
example: I surmise they left early.
secondExample: We can only surmise what happened.
useCase: Use it for an inferred guess.
origin: From Old French surmise, “accusation,” from Latin supermittere.

shortDefinition: A proposed explanation to be tested.
longDefinition: An educated guess put forward as a starting point for inquiry.
example: The data supported her hypothesis.
secondExample: It’s a hypothesis, not a conclusion.
useCase: Use it for a testable proposed explanation.
origin: From Greek hypothesis, “foundation, base.”

longDefinition: The attitudes and assumptions you bring to situations.
example: A growth mindset turns failure into data.
secondExample: Changing the outcome started with changing the mindset.
useCase: Use it for an established mental attitude.
origin: From mind plus set — a fixed mental posture.

shortDefinition: The power of reasoning and understanding.
longDefinition: The mind’s capacity for thought, knowledge, and judgment.
example: A formidable intellect behind a quiet manner.
secondExample: She engaged his intellect, not his ego.
useCase: Use it for reasoning power and brainpower.
origin: From Latin intellectus, “understanding,” from intellegere.

longDefinition: Writing in normal sentences and paragraphs rather than poetry.
example: Her prose is plain but luminous.
secondExample: He writes poetry but thinks in prose.
useCase: Use it for everyday, non-poetic writing.
origin: From Latin prosa, “straightforward (speech).”

shortDefinition: A told story, or the way events are framed.
longDefinition: An account of connected events; also the interpretation imposed on them.
example: The film follows two interwoven narratives.
secondExample: They lost control of the narrative.
useCase: Use it for a story or its framing.
origin: From Latin narrare, “to tell, relate.”

shortDefinition: Word choice, or clarity of speech.
longDefinition: The style of wording in writing, or how clearly one pronounces.
example: Her diction is precise and unfussy.
secondExample: Good diction carried to the back row.
useCase: Use it for word choice or clear enunciation.
origin: From Latin dictio, “a saying, word.”

shortDefinition: The vocabulary of a person, field, or language.
longDefinition: The full stock of words used in a language or by a group.
example: “Sonder” entered the modern lexicon online.
secondExample: Sailing has its own rich lexicon.
useCase: Use it for a body of vocabulary.
origin: From Greek lexikon, “of words,” from lexis, “word.”

shortDefinition: To restate something in your own words.
longDefinition: To express the same meaning in different wording.
example: Let me paraphrase what you just said.
secondExample: She paraphrased the ruling for the class.
useCase: Use it for restating in plainer words.
origin: From Greek paraphrasis, “a saying in other words.”

shortDefinition: To wander off the main topic.
longDefinition: To stray from the subject in speaking or writing.
example: Sorry to digress — back to the point.
secondExample: He digresses delightfully into old stories.
useCase: Use it for straying off-topic.
origin: From Latin digredi, “to step away.”

longDefinition: To declare something openly and with conviction.
example: They proclaimed victory before the count was done.
secondExample: She proclaimed her innocence to anyone listening.
useCase: Use it for a bold public announcement.
origin: From Latin proclamare, “to cry out.”

longDefinition: To declare confidently, or to stand up for a right or claim.
example: She asserted that the numbers were wrong.
secondExample: He learned to assert himself in meetings.
useCase: Use it for a confident claim or stand.
origin: From Latin asserere, “to claim, affirm.”

shortDefinition: To argue a point, or to compete.
longDefinition: To assert in argument, or to struggle against rivals or difficulty.
example: Critics contend the policy backfired.
secondExample: Three teams contend for the title.
useCase: Use it for arguing a case or competing.
origin: From Latin contendere, “to strive, stretch.”

longDefinition: To disprove an argument or accusation with evidence.
example: The study refuted the old assumption.
secondExample: He refuted the charge point by point.
useCase: Use it for disproving with evidence.
origin: From Latin refutare, “to drive back, rebut.”

shortDefinition: To admit something is true or lost.
longDefinition: To grant a point, or to acknowledge defeat.
example: She conceded he had a fair point.
secondExample: He conceded the race graciously.
useCase: Use it for granting or yielding a point.
origin: From Latin concedere, “to yield, give way.”

shortDefinition: A state of calm and peace.
longDefinition: Untroubled tranquility of mind or surroundings.
example: She found serenity in the early-morning quiet.
secondExample: There’s a serenity to the empty beach.
useCase: Use it for deep, settled calm.
origin: From Latin serenus, “clear, calm.”

longDefinition: A state of supreme joy and contentment.
example: A quiet evening at home is bliss.
secondExample: Pure bliss, that first day of vacation.
useCase: Use it for complete, blissful joy.
origin: From Old English bliss, “joy, gladness.”

longDefinition: A feeling of ecstatic, transporting happiness.
example: The crowd listened in rapture.
secondExample: Pure rapture lit her face.
useCase: Use it for transported, ecstatic joy.
origin: From Latin rapere, “to seize, carry off.”

longDefinition: Cheerful amusement, often shared and bubbling over.
example: The room filled with mirth.
secondExample: A note of mirth crept into her voice.
useCase: Use it for warm, laughing merriment.
origin: From Old English myrgth, “joy,” related to merry.

longDefinition: A lively sense of happiness, or something that brings it.
example: The puppy was a delight to everyone.
secondExample: She took delight in small surprises.
useCase: Use it for bright, lively pleasure.
origin: From Latin delectare, “to charm, please.”

shortDefinition: Severe pain or distress of mind.
longDefinition: Acute suffering, especially emotional torment.
example: The wait was sheer anguish.
secondExample: Anguish broke through her composure.
useCase: Use it for deep, piercing distress.
origin: From Latin angustia, “tightness, distress,” from angere, “to choke.”

longDefinition: A sinking conviction that nothing can improve.
example: Don’t give in to despair just yet.
secondExample: A flicker of hope cut through the despair.
useCase: Use it for hope’s lowest point.
origin: From Latin desperare, “to be without hope.”

longDefinition: Anxious foreboding about an approaching event.
example: A creeping dread filled the waiting room.
secondExample: He felt dread before every exam.
useCase: Use it for heavy, anticipatory fear.
origin: From Old English drædan, “to fear greatly.”

longDefinition: A shock of worry or disappointment that knocks you off balance.
example: To her dismay, the flight was cancelled.
secondExample: He stared at the bill in dismay.
useCase: Use it for a jolt of dismayed disappointment.
origin: From Old French desmaier, “to lose heart.”

shortDefinition: A feeling that something bad is coming.
longDefinition: An uneasy sense of approaching trouble, without clear cause.
example: A sense of foreboding hung over the house.
secondExample: She had a foreboding about the trip.
useCase: Use it for an ominous gut feeling.
origin: From fore-, “before,” plus bode, “to portend.”

longDefinition: A listless dissatisfaction born of having nothing that excites you.
example: A summer of ennui stretched ahead.
secondExample: Ennui set in by the third week off.
useCase: Use it for jaded, restless boredom.
origin: From French ennui, “annoyance,” from Latin in odio, “in hatred.”

shortDefinition: A single example used to study a whole.
longDefinition: An individual item taken as representative of a group or for examination.
example: A perfect specimen of the species.
secondExample: Each specimen was labeled and stored.
useCase: Use it for a representative sample.
origin: From Latin specimen, “a sign, example,” from specere, “to look.”

longDefinition: Transparent and pure; also having the structure of crystals.
example: The crystalline water showed every pebble.
secondExample: Her crystalline soprano filled the hall.
useCase: Use it for glassy, sparkling clarity.
origin: From Greek krystallos, “ice, rock crystal.”

shortDefinition: Speed, especially in a given direction.
longDefinition: The rate at which something moves; figuratively, momentum.
example: The ball gained velocity on the slope.
secondExample: The project picked up velocity after the demo.
useCase: Use it for speed and momentum.
origin: From Latin velocitas, from velox, “swift.”
